The SOP describes human-oversight procedures but does not specify override mechanisms required by Article 14 (the ability of natural persons to interrupt or override outputs and the conditions under which to do so). Transparency requirements are addressed generically without referencing the Article 13 specifics — capabilities, limitations, accuracy metrics, foreseeable risks, expected lifetime, and changes. Both topics are touched but neither is operationalised to the article's specificity.
question.notes: Ground truth: SOP-AIML-002 × EU AI Act = partial.

output.answer:
SOP-AIML-002 **partially, but not fully, meets** EU AI Act obligations for high-risk AI systems on **transparency (Article 13)** and **human oversight (Article 14)**.

| EU AI Act requirement | SOP-AIML-002 coverage | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| High-risk AI systems must be sufficiently transparent so deployers can interpret outputs and use the system appropriately. | SOP-AIML-002 requires Data Cards describing dataset composition, intended use, limitations, bias/fairness findings, quality results, and usage constraints. See SOP §§4.7, 5.8. | **Partial.** Helpful for data transparency, but not enough for system-output transparency required by EU AI Act Art. 13(1). |
| High-risk AI systems must be accompanied by clear instructions for use for deployers. | SOP-AIML-002 does not establish a system-level “instructions for use” document. It provides dataset documentation only. See SOP §5.8. | **Gap.** EU AI Act Art. 13(2) requires instructions for use that are concise, complete, correct, clear, relevant, accessible, and comprehensible to deployers. |
| Instructions must include provider identity/contact details. | SOP-AIML-002 identifies internal document owners and roles, but does not require provider identity/contact details in deployer-facing instructions. See SOP §§3.2, 11. | **Gap.** Required by EU AI Act Art. 13(3)(a). |
| Instructions must describe system characteristics, capabilities, limitations, intended purpose, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, foreseeable risk circumstances, explainability capabilities, group-specific performance, input-data specifications, and output interpretation information. | SOP-AIML-002 covers dataset intended use, composition, quality metrics, bias/fairness metrics, limitations, protected attributes, and input/training data information through Data Cards. See SOP §§5.3, 5.5, 5.8. | **Partial.** It covers training-data specifications and some group/bias information, aligning partly with EU AI Act Art. 13(3)(b)(v)–(vi), but it does not require system-level accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, explainability, or output-interpretation information required by Art. 13(3)(b)(ii)–(iv), (vii). |
| Instructions must describe predetermined system changes, human oversight measures, computational/hardware resources, expected lifetime, and maintenance measures. | SOP-AIML-002 addresses dataset versioning, maintenance, refresh triggers, and review dates, but not system-level predetermined changes, human oversight measures for deployers, compute/hardware requirements, expected system lifetime, or maintenance/care measures. See SOP §§5.6, 5.8. | **Gap/partial.** Dataset lifecycle controls partially support documentation, but EU AI Act Art. 13(3)(c)–(e) requires system-level information. |
| High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed with appropriate human-machine interface tools so natural persons can effectively oversee them during use. | SOP-AIML-002 requires human review in data sourcing, quality sign-off, bias evaluation, labeling validation, and automated quality checks as decision support rather than sole arbiters. See SOP §4.8. | **Partial.** This supports human oversight of data processes, but not human-machine interface tools for oversight of the AI system in use as required by EU AI Act Art. 14(1). |
| Human oversight must prevent or minimize risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights during intended use or foreseeable misuse. | SOP-AIML-002 includes bias assessments, data quality thresholds, escalation, stop-use orders for critical data issues, and drift monitoring. See SOP §§5.5, 7.2, 8.4. | **Partial.** These controls reduce data-related risk, but Article 14(2) concerns oversight of the system during use, not only dataset governance. |
| Oversight measures must enable overseers to understand system capacities/limitations, monitor anomalies, avoid automation bias, interpret outputs, disregard/override/reverse outputs, and intervene or stop the system safely. | SOP-AIML-002 includes training on bias awareness and data management, and requires human reviewers for data gates. See SOP §§4.8, 9.1. It does not define deployer/clinical-user oversight workflows, output interpretation tools, automation-bias warnings for end users, override/reversal authority, or stop-button/safe-halt procedures for the AI system. | **Gap.** EU AI Act Art. 14(4)(a)–(e) requires these system-use oversight capabilities. |
| For certain biometric high-risk systems, decisions based on identification must be verified by at least two natural persons. | SOP-AIML-002 does not address biometric identification systems or dual verification of AI outputs; its dual annotation requirement applies to training labels for high-risk datasets. See SOP §5.4.3. | **Not addressed / likely not applicable unless Meridian deploys Annex III point 1(a) biometric identification systems.** EU AI Act Art. 14(5). |

**Bottom line:** SOP-AIML-002 is a strong **training data governance** SOP and provides meaningful support for EU AI Act Article 13 transparency around training, validation, test data, bias, quality, provenance, and dataset limitations. However, it does **not** fully satisfy Article 13 because it lacks deployer-facing system instructions for use and system-level information on performance, risks, output interpretation, oversight, resources, lifetime, and maintenance. It also does **not** fully satisfy Article 14 because it focuses on human oversight of **data management workflows**, not human oversight of the **high-risk AI system during use**, including HMI tools, override/reversal, automation-bias controls, and stop/safe-halt mechanisms.

To reach full alignment, Meridian should supplement SOP-AIML-002 with a high-risk AI system instructions-for-use and human-oversight SOP, likely under SOP-AIML-004 or SOP-AIML-001, covering the missing Article 13 and Article 14 elements.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-AIML-009 (Algorithmic Bias Detection and Mitigation) compliant with the EU AI Act's high-risk classification, conformity assessment, and registration obligations?
question.expected_answer: Gap. SOP-AIML-009 has no AI system risk classification procedure or conformity assessment process (Article 43), is missing a fundamental-rights impact assessment for high-risk systems (Article 27), and makes no provision for AI system registration in the EU database (Article 49). For a bias-detection SOP intended for high-risk AI use cases, these are core obligations.
question.notes: Ground truth: SOP-AIML-009 × EU AI Act = gap.

output.answer:
No. SOP-AIML-009 is **not fully compliant** with the EU AI Act obligations for **high-risk classification, conformity assessment, and registration**. It contains strong bias-governance controls that would support parts of EU AI Act readiness, but it does **not establish the required EU AI Act legal classification workflow, conformity assessment workflow, CE/EU declaration steps, or EU database registration process**.

| EU AI Act obligation | EU AI Act requirement | SOP-AIML-009 assessment | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-risk classification** | AI systems are high-risk if they are safety components/products covered by EU harmonisation legislation and subject to third-party conformity assessment, or if they fall within Annex III high-risk categories. EU AI Act, **Article 6(1)–(2)**. Medical devices and in vitro diagnostic medical devices are specifically referenced as product areas where AI may be high-risk when relevant conformity assessment applies. EU AI Act, **Recital 30**. Creditworthiness/credit scoring systems are high-risk because they affect access to financial resources or essential services. EU AI Act, **Recital 37**. | SOP-AIML-009 has internal “Critical/High/Standard/Low” model risk tiers and covers clinical AI, diagnostic imaging, adverse event prediction, HealthPay credit scoring/lending, and consequential decision models. SOP §§1.2, 2, 4.3, 5.4. However, it does **not map internal risk tiers to EU AI Act Article 6, Annex III, or EU harmonisation/product-law triggers**. | Add a mandatory EU AI Act classification step that records: intended purpose, provider/deployer role, Article 6(1) product/safety-component analysis, Annex III category analysis, and rationale for high-risk/non-high-risk status. |
| **Provider/deployer role determination** | Providers of high-risk AI systems must meet Article 16 obligations, including compliance with high-risk requirements, QMS, documentation, logs, conformity assessment, EU declaration of conformity, CE marking, and registration. EU AI Act, **Article 16(a)–(i)**. Deployers have separate duties, including using systems according to instructions, assigning competent human oversight, ensuring relevant/representative input data where under their control, monitoring, and reporting risks. EU AI Act, **Article 26(1)–(5)**. | SOP applies to systems “developed, procured, or deployed,” but does not distinguish when Meridian is a **provider**, **deployer**, or user of third-party high-risk AI. SOP §§1.2, 6.2 A-03. It also excludes some legacy third-party tools where Meridian cannot influence design, but EU AI Act deployer obligations can still apply to high-risk AI systems in use. EU AI Act, **Article 26**. | Add role-based obligations for provider, deployer, importer/distributor if applicable, and require vendor/procured high-risk systems to be classified and governed even where Meridian cannot retrain or redesign them. |
| **Conformity assessment before market/service** | Providers must ensure high-risk AI systems undergo the relevant conformity assessment before being placed on the market or put into service. EU AI Act, **Article 16(f)** and **Article 43**. For Annex III points 2–8 systems, providers generally follow the internal-control conformity assessment procedure; for certain product-regulated systems, conformity assessment is integrated with the applicable EU product legislation. EU AI Act, **Article 43(2)–(3)**. | SOP has an internal pre-deployment review, BiasComplianceCheck, model cards, fairness assessments, CAIO/IRB approvals, and monitoring. SOP §§5.4–5.6. These are useful but are **not an EU AI Act conformity assessment procedure**. The SOP does not require Annex VI/internal-control evidence, Annex VII/notified-body assessment where applicable, harmonised standards/common specifications analysis, substantial modification reassessment, or integration with medical-device/product conformity assessment. | Add an EU AI Act conformity assessment procedure with required evidence, responsible owner, timing before placing on market/putting into service, applicable module, notified-body involvement where required, and reassessment after substantial modifications. |
| **Quality management and technical documentation supporting conformity** | Providers must maintain a QMS documented in policies/procedures/instructions, including regulatory compliance strategy, design controls, development/QA controls, validation/testing, standards, and data-management procedures. EU AI Act, **Article 17(1)(a)–(f)**. Technical documentation must be prepared before market/service and kept up to date to demonstrate compliance, including Annex IV elements. EU AI Act, **Article 11(1)**. | SOP includes many QMS-like elements for bias testing: data preparation, proxy detection, subgroup metrics, thresholds, remediation, model cards, approvals, monitoring, and training. SOP §§5–9. But it is limited to algorithmic bias and does not require full EU AI Act technical documentation or a regulatory compliance strategy for all high-risk requirements, including transparency, human oversight, robustness, accuracy, cybersecurity, logging, instructions for use, and post-market monitoring. EU AI Act, **Articles 8–17**. | Expand the SOP or cross-reference a master AI Act QMS/technical-documentation SOP covering all high-risk requirements, not only bias/fairness. |
| **EU declaration of conformity and CE marking** | Providers of high-risk AI systems must draw up an EU declaration of conformity and affix CE marking to indicate conformity. EU AI Act, **Article 16(g)–(h)**; EU declaration requirements are addressed in **Article 47**, and CE marking in **Article 48** per the retrieved Article 16 text. | SOP does not mention EU declarations of conformity, CE marking, maintaining declarations, or who signs them. SOP §5.4 only addresses internal approval and bias-governance tags. | Add mandatory EU declaration and CE marking steps for applicable high-risk AI systems, including accountable signatory, retention, update triggers, and linkage to technical documentation. |
| **Registration in EU database** | Providers must comply with registration obligations for high-risk AI systems. EU AI Act, **Article 16(i)**. For high-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2), registration must occur before placing on the market or putting into service. EU AI Act, **Article 51** in retrieved text. The EU database contains provider-entered information listed in Annex VIII and is publicly accessible. EU AI Act, **Article 60(1)–(3)**. | SOP contains no EU database registration requirement, no Annex VIII data collection, no timing control before deployment, and no assigned registration owner. SOP §§5.4, 7.2. | Add a registration gate for Article 6(2)/Annex III high-risk AI systems before market/service, with required Annex VIII fields, evidence of submission, owner, and deployment block if registration is incomplete. |
| **Fundamental rights impact assessment connection** | Certain deployers of Article 6(2) high-risk AI systems must perform a fundamental rights impact assessment before deployment, including process description, affected groups, specific risks of harm, oversight measures, and mitigation steps. EU AI Act, **Article 27(1)(a)–(f)**. | SOP has bias scoping, affected protected attributes, fairness metrics, human oversight, and DPIA/privacy consultation triggers. SOP §§4.3, 5.1, 5.3, 5.5. However, it does not require an EU AI Act FRIA where applicable. | Add FRIA trigger logic for applicable high-risk deployments, especially HealthPay credit/lending and essential-service use cases, and link FRIA outputs to conformity/registration records. |

### Bottom line

SOP-AIML-009 is a strong **algorithmic bias detection and mitigation SOP**, but it is **not an EU AI Act high-risk compliance SOP**. It partially supports high-risk compliance through fairness testing, model cards, human oversight, monitoring, and internal approvals, but it lacks the legally required EU AI Act mechanisms for:

1. Article 6 / Annex III high-risk classification;
2. provider vs deployer role determination;
3. Article 43 conformity assessment;
4. EU declaration of conformity and CE marking;
5. EU database registration before market/service; and
6. full high-risk technical documentation/QMS coverage beyond bias.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-AIML-009 satisfy NIST AI RMF's governance and risk-management expectations?
question.expected_answer: Gap. The SOP does not provide a structured approach to AI trustworthiness across the seven characteristics, has no GOVERN function (no AI governance body or accountability structure), and does not reference or adopt an AI risk-management framework. Despite addressing bias detection — a MEASURE concern — the SOP lacks the surrounding GOVERN/MAP/MANAGE scaffolding that makes the framework actionable.
question.notes: Ground truth: SOP-AIML-009 × NIST AI RMF = gap.

output.answer:
No. **SOP-AIML-009 partially satisfies NIST AI RMF 1.0 expectations**: it is strong for **fairness / harmful-bias governance, measurement, monitoring, remediation, training, and approvals**, but it does **not fully cover the broader NIST AI RMF governance and AI-risk-management scope** across all trustworthy AI characteristics, stakeholder impact mapping, residual-risk treatment, decommissioning, and enterprise-level AI inventory.

NIST AI RMF is also **not intended as a checklist or standalone compliance certification mechanism**; it is meant to be integrated into enterprise risk management. (NIST AI RMF 1.0, Part 1 §2 “Scope”; Part 2 §5 “AI RMF Core”.)

| NIST AI RMF expectation | SOP-AIML-009 assessment |
|---|---|
| **Governance policies, roles, accountability, and risk culture.** NIST expects organizational policies and procedures for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks; risk-tolerance-based activities; ongoing review; clear roles; and training. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 §5.1, GOVERN 1.1–1.5 and GOVERN 2.1–2.2.) | **Mostly satisfied for bias/fairness.** The SOP defines enterprise-wide scope, RACI roles, CAIO/IRB approvals, policy statements, monthly/quarterly/annual reporting, exceptions, escalation, and role-based training. (SOP-AIML-009 §§1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9.) |
| **Trustworthy AI coverage.** NIST frames trustworthy AI broadly: valid and reliable, safe, secure and resilient, accountable and transparent, explainable and interpretable, privacy-enhanced, and fair with harmful bias managed. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 Part 1 §3 “AI Risks and Trustworthiness”.) | **Gap.** SOP-AIML-009 focuses on algorithmic bias, fairness metrics, disparate impact, privacy during bias testing, model cards, and human oversight. It does not itself establish complete procedures for validity/reliability, safety, security/resilience, explainability beyond “salient factors,” or broader transparency/accountability outside bias controls. (SOP-AIML-009 §§4.1–4.5, 5.1–5.6.) |
| **AI inventory and lifecycle governance.** NIST expects mechanisms to inventory AI systems and procedures to decommission or phase out AI systems safely. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 §5.1, GOVERN 1.6–1.7.) | **Partial/gap.** The SOP uses MLflow Model Registry tags, Model Cards, and deployment gates, but it does not define an enterprise AI-system inventory process, ownership of inventory completeness, or a safe decommissioning/phase-out procedure. (SOP-AIML-009 §§4.5, 5.4, 6.1 T-02.) |
| **Context and impact mapping.** NIST expects intended purpose, deployment context, laws/norms, users, AI tasks/methods, system knowledge limits, targeted scope, human oversight, third-party component risks, and impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society to be documented. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 §5.2, MAP 1.1, MAP 2.1–2.3, MAP 3.3–3.5, MAP 4.1–4.2, MAP 5.1–5.2.) | **Partially satisfied.** The SOP requires decision-context definition, population served, protected-attribute/proxy identification, fairness metrics/thresholds, data lineage, proxy detection, human-in-the-loop for high-risk consequential decisions, and vendor fairness assessment. (SOP-AIML-009 §§4.3, 5.1, 5.2, 6.2 A-03.) It does **not** require full documentation of non-bias impacts, foreseeable misuse, affected-community feedback, benefits/costs beyond fairness, or broader third-party legal/IP/component risk mapping. |
| **Measurement and TEVV.** NIST expects appropriate methods and metrics, documentation of test sets/tools, regular assessment and updating, evaluation for trustworthy characteristics, and independent or non-frontline assessment where appropriate. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 §5.3, MEASURE 1.1–1.3 and MEASURE 2.1–2.3.) | **Strong for bias; partial overall.** The SOP requires pre-defined fairness metrics/thresholds, validation-set subgroup metrics, MLflow artifacts, Model Cards, automated production monitoring, drift alerting, and periodic revalidation. (SOP-AIML-009 §§5.1, 5.3, 5.4, 5.6, 7.1.) However, its measurement program is limited to fairness/bias and does not require measurement of all NIST trustworthy-AI characteristics. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 Part 1 §3; SOP-AIML-009 §§5.3, 5.6.) |
| **Risk response and management.** NIST expects go/no-go decisions, prioritized risk treatment based on impact/likelihood/resources, documented responses such as mitigation/transfer/avoidance/acceptance, residual-risk documentation, and procedures for unknown risks. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 §5.4, MANAGE 1.1–1.4 and MANAGE 2.1–2.3.) | **Mostly satisfied for bias incidents; partial overall.** The SOP blocks deployment when fairness thresholds fail, requires remediation hierarchy, CAIO/IRB approvals, exception requests with risk assessment and expiry, P0 bias-drift incidents, escalation, and scheduled revalidation. (SOP-AIML-009 §§5.3–5.6, 8.1–8.3.) It does not clearly require residual-risk documentation for downstream users/acquirers, explicit risk acceptance/transfer criteria, comparison with non-AI alternatives, or recovery/communication procedures for broader unknown AI risks. |
| **Stakeholder and affected-community input.** NIST expects mapping and measurement to incorporate relevant AI actors and, where needed, domain experts, users, external AI actors, and affected communities. (NIST AI RMF 1.0 §5.2, MAP 5.2; §5.3, MEASURE 1.3.) | **Gap/partial.** The SOP includes internal stakeholders such as Product, Data Science, Compliance, Privacy/DPO, General Counsel, CAIO, VP Engineering, and IRB, but it does not establish processes for affected-community, patient, borrower, clinician-user, or other external stakeholder engagement in bias assessment or impact review. (SOP-AIML-009 §§3, 5.1, 6.2, 7.2.) |

**Bottom line:** SOP-AIML-009 is a mature **bias detection and mitigation SOP** and aligns well with NIST AI RMF expectations for the “fair with harmful bias managed” characteristic. But as a standalone SOP, it does **not** fully satisfy NIST AI RMF’s broader governance and risk-management expectations across the full AI lifecycle and all trustworthy-AI risk dimensions. To close the gaps, Meridian should add or cross-reference enforceable procedures for enterprise AI inventory, decommissioning, full impact mapping, affected-stakeholder engagement, non-bias TEVV, residual-risk documentation, broader incident recovery/communications, and non-AI alternative analysis.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-AIML-005 (Feature Engineering and Feature Store Operations) compliant with NIST AI RMF's expectations for an AI inventory and risk-management framework?
question.expected_answer: Gap. The SOP does not adopt or reference an AI risk-management framework, does not maintain an AI system inventory mapped to organisational risk, and has no bias/fairness/equity testing methodology. Feature engineering directly affects MEASURE-stage trustworthiness (bias, validity), so the absence of these structures is a material gap.
question.notes: Ground truth: SOP-AIML-005 × NIST AI RMF = gap.

output.answer:
## Assessment

SOP-AIML-005 is **partially compliant** with NIST AI RMF 1.0 expectations for AI inventory and AI risk-management framework support. It provides strong feature-level governance, documentation, validation, monitoring, access control, and deprecation controls, but it does **not** establish a complete enterprise AI system inventory or a full AI risk-management framework across the Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions.

## What SOP-AIML-005 does well

| NIST AI RMF expectation | SOP evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| AI risk-management processes should be established through policies, procedures, and controls based on organizational risk priorities. | SOP-AIML-005 establishes mandatory feature store registration, Feature Specs, pipeline-as-code, training-serving consistency, point-in-time correctness, data classification handling, CI/CD controls, and exception handling. | Strong for feature engineering controls, but not a complete AI risk framework. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.4; SOP §§4.1–4.6, 5.2, 5.3, 8. |
| Roles and responsibilities for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks should be documented. | The SOP defines roles such as Feature Store Owner, Feature Store Platform Lead, Feature Author, Feature Reviewer, Data Steward, Model Validation Engineer, MLOps Engineer, and Release Manager, plus a RACI matrix. | Largely aligned for feature operations. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 2.1; SOP §3, §3.1. |
| Personnel should receive AI risk-management training consistent with their duties. | The SOP requires SOP familiarization, feature store SDK training, data leakage prevention training, PHI/PII handling training, and HealthPay validation training, with LMS tracking. | Aligned for the feature engineering lifecycle. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 2.2; SOP §9. |
| Mechanisms should exist to inventory AI systems and resource them according to risk priorities. | The SOP mandates that all production, staging, and pre-production ML features be registered in the Feature Store and that Feature Specs identify associated models or use cases. HealthPay feature groups are added to the HealthPay Model Inventory. | Partial. This is a feature inventory and limited model linkage, not a full AI system inventory across all AI systems. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.6; SOP §§4.1, 5.2.1, 5.6.1. |
| AI components, including third-party data or software, should have mapped risks and controls. | The SOP requires source data documentation, PHI/PII assessment, Data Steward review, access controls, and externally sourced feature coverage. | Partial. It addresses data classification and access, but does not require documented legal/IP risk mapping or third-party software/data risk assessment for all components. NIST AI RMF MAP 4.1, MAP 4.2; SOP §§1.2, 5.1.2, 5.2.1, 6.1. |
| Measurement methods and metrics should be selected and documented for identified risks. | The SOP requires point-in-time validation, schema validation, unit test coverage, training-serving consistency tests, drift baselines, PSI-based drift monitoring, SLA metrics, and operational dashboards. | Strong for feature validity, leakage, drift, and operational reliability. NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.1, MEASURE 2.1; SOP §§5.2.1, 5.3.4, 5.5, 6.4, 7. |
| Independent or non-front-line review should be involved in assessments. | The SOP requires Feature Reviewer approval, Data Steward review for PHI/PII features, and Model Validation Engineer review for HealthPay models. | Partially aligned; independent review is strongest for HealthPay but not uniformly required across all high-risk uses. NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.3; SOP §§5.2.2, 5.6.1. |
| AI risks should be managed through monitoring, incident response, and ongoing improvement. | The SOP includes drift alerts, pipeline failure monitoring, weekly reviews, monthly reporting, exception logs, escalation paths, and retraining evaluation triggers. | Good feature-level risk management. NIST AI RMF MANAGE 2.2, MANAGE 2.3; SOP §§6.4, 7.3, 7.4, 8. |
| AI systems should have safe decommissioning and phase-out processes. | The SOP includes a feature deprecation process with 90-day notice, downstream model identification, online store removal, and offline retention for auditability. | Aligned for feature groups, but not for full AI systems/models. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.7; SOP §5.7. |

## Key gaps against NIST AI RMF expectations

1. **No enterprise-wide AI system inventory.**  
   NIST expects mechanisms to inventory AI systems, resourced according to organizational risk priorities. SOP-AIML-005 inventories Feature Groups and links some features to associated models, but it does not require a complete AI system inventory containing model/system owner, lifecycle stage, intended purpose, deployment context, risk tier, users, affected populations, dependencies, and retirement status. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.6; SOP §§4.1, 5.2.1, 5.6.1.

2. **Risk prioritization is not formalized across AI systems.**  
   NIST expects AI risks to be prioritized based on impact, likelihood, and available resources, with documented responses for high-priority risks. SOP-AIML-005 has feature-level controls and exception handling, but it does not define an enterprise AI risk taxonomy, risk scoring method, risk tolerance, or prioritization process across AI systems. NIST AI RMF MANAGE 1.2, MANAGE 1.3; SOP §§6.4, 8.

3. **Residual risk documentation is missing.**  
   NIST expects negative residual risks to downstream acquirers and end users to be documented. SOP-AIML-005 requires validation artifacts and drift monitoring, but it does not require residual risk statements for feature groups, models, users, patients, members, or downstream consumers. NIST AI RMF MANAGE 1.4; SOP §§5.5, 5.6, 6.4.

4. **Impact mapping is incomplete.**  
   NIST expects impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society to be characterized, including likelihood and magnitude. SOP-AIML-005 focuses on technical risks such as data leakage, drift, freshness, PHI/PII classification, and training-serving skew, but it does not require impact assessment for affected individuals or groups. NIST AI RMF MAP 5.1; SOP §§5.1.2, 5.5, 6.4.

5. **Trustworthiness dimensions are only partially covered.**  
   NIST frames trustworthy AI around characteristics including validity/reliability, safety, security/resilience, accountability/transparency, explainability/interoperability, privacy enhancement, and harmful bias management. SOP-AIML-005 covers validity/reliability through point-in-time correctness, schema enforcement, unit testing, and drift monitoring; it covers privacy/security through PHI/PII tagging, IAM, encryption, and access controls; but it does not require feature-level bias assessment, explainability documentation, safety analysis, or human-oversight analysis. NIST AI RMF Part 1 §3.1–§3.7; SOP §§4.5, 5.3.3, 5.5, 6.1, 6.3, 6.4.

6. **Third-party and component risk mapping is underdeveloped.**  
   NIST expects risks and benefits to be mapped for all components of the AI system, including third-party software and data, and expects legal/IP risks to be documented. SOP-AIML-005 covers externally sourced features and source data documentation, but it does not require third-party data provenance review, vendor risk review, licensing review, IP rights assessment, or component-level legal risk mapping. NIST AI RMF MAP 4.1, MAP 4.2; SOP §§1.2, 5.2.1, 10.2.

7. **Independent assessment is not consistently risk-based.**  
   NIST expects internal experts who were not front-line developers, independent assessors, domain experts, users, external AI actors, and affected communities to be consulted as necessary according to organizational risk tolerance. SOP-AIML-005 requires Feature Reviewer and Data Steward review, and HealthPay-specific Model Validation Engineer review, but it does not define when broader domain expert, user, independent, or affected-community consultation is required. NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.3; SOP §§5.2.2, 5.6.1.

## Recommended remediation

To bring SOP-AIML-005 closer to NIST AI RMF alignment, Meridian should add or cross-reference the following controls:

1. **AI inventory linkage requirement:** require every Feature Group to link to an enterprise AI system inventory record, not only to associated models or HealthPay Model Inventory entries. The inventory should include system owner, intended purpose, lifecycle stage, deployment environment, user group, affected population, risk tier, data sources, third-party components, model dependencies, monitoring status, and decommissioning status. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.6; SOP §§5.2.1, 5.6.1.

2. **Risk taxonomy and scoring:** require Feature Specs or associated Model Development Plans to document risk categories, likelihood, impact, severity, risk owner, controls, residual risk, and risk acceptance. NIST AI RMF MANAGE 1.2–1.4; SOP §5.2.1.

3. **Impact assessment:** add an “AI impact and stakeholder assessment” section to the Feature Spec for features used in clinical, financial, or member-facing decisions. This should identify affected individuals/groups, potential harms, benefits, and downstream uses. NIST AI RMF MAP 5.1; SOP §5.2.1.

4. **Third-party component review:** require documentation of third-party data, software, open-source components, licensing/IP considerations, and vendor/component risk controls for externally sourced features or feature tooling. NIST AI RMF MAP 4.1, MAP 4.2; SOP §§1.2, 10.2.

5. **Expanded trustworthiness testing:** add feature-level prompts for bias/proxy risk, explainability, safety relevance, privacy-enhancing techniques, and security abuse cases where applicable. NIST AI RMF Part 1 §3.1–§3.7; SOP §§5.2.1, 5.5, 6.

6. **Risk-based independent review:** require independent validation beyond HealthPay for high-risk AI systems or features used in clinical, financial, eligibility, safety, or patient-impacting contexts. NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.3; SOP §5.6.1.

## Bottom line

SOP-AIML-005 is a mature and well-controlled **feature engineering and feature store operations SOP**. It materially supports NIST AI RMF implementation by strengthening feature documentation, traceability, leakage prevention, data governance, validation, drift monitoring, and operational controls. However, it is **not sufficient by itself** to satisfy NIST AI RMF expectations for an AI inventory and enterprise AI risk-management framework because it does not establish a complete AI system inventory, formal risk scoring/prioritization, impact assessment, residual risk documentation, or full trustworthy-AI assessment across all AI systems.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-CLIN-001 (Clinical Decision Support System Validation) meet HIPAA's access-control and breach-notification requirements?
question.expected_answer: Partial. The SOP references access controls but does not operationalise HIPAA's minimum-necessary standard (§164.502(b)) at the role-and-purpose level. Breach notification is mentioned but the 60-day individual-notification timeline (§164.404) and HHS-reporting cadence (§164.408) are not specified. These elements are addressed but not implemented to the standard's specificity.

output.answer:
No. **SOP-CLIN-001 partially addresses HIPAA access-control expectations, but it does not fully meet HIPAA’s access-control requirements and it has major gaps against HIPAA breach-notification requirements.** It appears to rely on other documents—especially SOP-CLIN-003 and SOP-IS-001—for full HIPAA implementation, but those controls are not contained in this SOP.

## 1. HIPAA access-control assessment

### HIPAA requirements

HIPAA requires covered entities and business associates to implement technical policies and procedures for systems that maintain ePHI so that access is limited to persons or software programs with granted access rights. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(1)**. HIPAA also requires: **unique user identification** and **emergency access procedures** as required implementation specifications, and **automatic logoff** and **encryption/decryption** as addressable implementation specifications. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(i)–(iv)**. HIPAA further requires policies and procedures for authorizing access to ePHI consistent with the Privacy Rule. **45 CFR § 164.308(a)(4)(i)**, with access authorization as an addressable implementation specification under **45 CFR § 164.308(a)(4)(ii)(B)**.

### What SOP-CLIN-001 includes

SOP-CLIN-001 contains some relevant access-control safeguards:

- The validation lockbox is stored in a “read-only, access-controlled S3 bucket,” and access logging is enabled. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 5.2**.
- Validation runs occur in an isolated AWS sub-account with no connectivity to the production EHR-integrated environment. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.1**.
- Access to the lockbox S3 bucket and validation-runner EC2 instance is logged, and access is restricted by IAM roles to the Lead Data Scientist and Biostatistician during the active run phase. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.1**.
- Quarterly reviews are required for accounts with `ReadWrite` permissions to the relevant AWS organization controls, and unauthorized access triggers incident response under SOP-CLIN-003. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.2**.
- Vendor clinical readers/CROs must undergo annual SOC 2 Type II review and execute a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.2**.

These provisions support HIPAA’s general access-limitation concept under **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(1)** and information access management under **45 CFR § 164.308(a)(4)(i)**.

### Access-control gaps

However, SOP-CLIN-001 does **not** fully specify the HIPAA access-control requirements:

| HIPAA requirement | SOP status | Gap |
|---|---:|---|
| Access only for authorized persons/software. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(1)** | Partial | SOP limits certain validation access to IAM roles, but does not define a full access authorization workflow for ePHI systems or production CDSS access. **SOP-CLIN-001 §§ 5.2, 6.1, 6.2** |
| Access authorization policies/procedures. **45 CFR § 164.308(a)(4)(i), (a)(4)(ii)(B)** | Partial | Quarterly review of `ReadWrite` access is present, but the SOP does not define request, approval, provisioning, modification, periodic recertification for all access levels, or deprovisioning. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.2** |
| Unique user identification. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(i)** | Gap | The SOP mentions IAM roles and access logs, but does not require unique user IDs for each user accessing ePHI or validation systems. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.1** |
| Emergency access procedure. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(ii)** | Gap | The SOP contains escalation and safety-disabling procedures, but it does not define emergency access to necessary ePHI. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 8.2** |
| Automatic logoff. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(iii)** | Gap | The SOP does not specify inactivity timeouts or automatic session termination for systems containing ePHI. |
| Encryption/decryption mechanism. **45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(iv)** | Gap/unclear | The SOP references de-identification, S3 lockbox controls, and isolated environments, but does not require encryption at rest or in transit for ePHI, nor key-management responsibilities. **SOP-CLIN-001 §§ 5.2, 6.1** |
| Audit controls. **45 CFR § 164.312(b)** | Partial | The SOP requires CloudTrail/S3 access logging and append-only validation logs, but does not state how logs are reviewed, retained, monitored, or escalated for all systems containing ePHI. **SOP-CLIN-001 §§ 5.2, 5.4, 6.1** |

**Conclusion on access control:** SOP-CLIN-001 is **partially aligned** with HIPAA access-control safeguards, mainly for validation lockboxes and validation execution environments. It is not sufficient as a complete HIPAA access-control SOP because it omits explicit unique user identification, emergency access, automatic logoff, encryption/decryption, and a complete access authorization/deprovisioning process required or contemplated by **45 CFR §§ 164.308(a)(4) and 164.312(a)**.

## 2. HIPAA breach-notification assessment

### HIPAA requirements

HIPAA requires a covered entity to notify each affected individual after discovery of a breach of unsecured PHI. **45 CFR § 164.404(a)(1)**. A breach is treated as discovered on the first day it is known, or by reasonable diligence would have been known, to the covered entity. **45 CFR § 164.404(a)(2)**. Individual notice must be provided without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery. **45 CFR § 164.404(b)**. The notice must include, to the extent possible, a description of what happened, the types of PHI involved, steps individuals should take, what the entity is doing to investigate/mitigate/prevent further breaches, and contact procedures. **45 CFR § 164.404(c)(1)(A)–(E)**.

For breaches involving more than 500 residents of a state or jurisdiction, HIPAA requires notice to prominent media outlets without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery. **45 CFR § 164.406(a)–(b)**. HIPAA also requires notice to the Secretary of HHS; breaches involving 500 or more individuals must be reported contemporaneously with individual notice, and breaches involving fewer than 500 individuals must be logged and reported no later than 60 days after the end of the calendar year. **45 CFR § 164.408(a)–(c)**. If the organization is acting as a business associate, it must notify the covered entity of a breach without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery, and provide the identity of affected individuals and other available information needed for individual notice. **45 CFR § 164.410(a)–(c)**.

### What SOP-CLIN-001 includes

SOP-CLIN-001 has only a high-level breach reference:

- A data breach impacting PHI triggers the “Breach Response Protocol,” requiring prompt notification to relevant authorities in accordance with applicable timelines. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 8.2**.
- The SOP references related policies, including SOP-CLIN-003, CDSS Data Governance and Access Control Standard, and SOP-IS-001, Information Security Incident Response Plan. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 10.1**.

### Breach-notification gaps

The SOP does **not** contain the core HIPAA breach-notification procedures:

| HIPAA requirement | SOP status | Gap |
|---|---:|---|
| Notify affected individuals after breach of unsecured PHI. **45 CFR § 164.404(a)(1)** | Gap | SOP only says “relevant authorities,” not affected individuals. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 8.2** |
| Discovery standard. **45 CFR § 164.404(a)(2)** | Gap | SOP does not define when a breach is deemed discovered or whose knowledge triggers the clock. |
| 60-day outer deadline for individual notice. **45 CFR § 164.404(b)** | Gap | SOP says “prompt notification” and “applicable timelines,” but does not state the HIPAA 60-calendar-day deadline. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 8.2** |
| Required content of individual notice. **45 CFR § 164.404(c)(1)(A)–(E)** | Gap | SOP does not require description of incident, PHI types, individual protective steps, mitigation/prevention actions, or contact procedures. |
| Media notice for >500 residents of a state/jurisdiction. **45 CFR § 164.406(a)–(c)** | Gap | SOP does not mention media notification thresholds or timing. |
| HHS Secretary notice. **45 CFR § 164.408(a)–(c)** | Gap | SOP does not specify HHS notification, contemporaneous notice for 500+ individuals, or annual logging/reporting for fewer than 500. |
| Business associate notice to covered entity. **45 CFR § 164.410(a)–(c)** | Gap | SOP requires BAAs for vendors but does not define Meridian’s own business-associate breach notice duties or vendor-to-Meridian breach reporting content/timing. **SOP-CLIN-001 § 6.2** |

**Conclusion on breach notification:** SOP-CLIN-001 does **not** meet HIPAA breach-notification requirements on its own. Its single reference to a Breach Response Protocol is insufficient because it does not operationalize individual notice, HHS notice, media notice, discovery rules, required content, methods, or business-associate notification requirements under **45 CFR §§ 164.404, 164.406, 164.408, and 164.410**.

## Overall conclusion

SOP-CLIN-001 is a clinical validation SOP with some useful security controls for validation data, including access-controlled S3 lockboxes, IAM role restrictions, logging, quarterly access review, de-identification, and vendor BAAs. **SOP-CLIN-001 §§ 5.2, 6.1, 6.2**. Those controls provide partial support for HIPAA access control under **45 CFR §§ 164.308(a)(4) and 164.312(a)**. But the SOP does not fully define required HIPAA access-control implementation specifications, and it lacks the detailed breach-notification procedures required by **45 CFR §§ 164.404, 164.406, 164.408, and 164.410**.

Recommended remediation: incorporate or cross-reference binding procedures that expressly cover unique user IDs, emergency access, automatic logoff, encryption/decryption, access approval and termination, breach discovery, 60-day notification deadlines, individual/media/HHS notices, notice content, substitute notice, business-associate notification, and breach documentation/logging.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-COPS-001 (Customer Data Handling) compliant with GDPR's requirements on lawful basis, consent, and international transfers?
question.expected_answer: Gap. Cross-border data transfers occur with no Chapter V safeguards documented, there is no documented Article 6 lawful basis per processing activity, and consent mechanisms (opt-in capture, withdrawal, records) are missing. For a customer-data SOP this is the GDPR foundation; its absence is non-compliance, not partial.

question.question: Does SOP-COPS-001 meet SOC 2 requirements for change management, monitoring, and vulnerability management?
question.expected_answer: Gap. There is no formal change-management process meeting CC8.1 (changes deployed without approval workflows), continuous monitoring per CC7.2/CC7.3 is missing (security events not aggregated or reviewed), and there is no penetration-testing or vulnerability-scanning programme tied to CC7.1/CC7.2. Multiple Common Criteria are unmet.

output.answer:
SOP-COPS-001 **partially meets** SOC 2 expectations for monitoring, but it does **not sufficiently meet SOC 2 change management or vulnerability management requirements** as a standalone SOP.

| Area | SOC 2 requirement | SOP-COPS-001 coverage | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Change management | SOC 2 CC8.1 requires changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures to be authorized, designed/developed securely, documented, tracked, tested, approved, and deployed with segregation of responsibilities. | SOP-COPS-001 includes revision history and an exception process, but it does not define a formal change management workflow for production/system changes. | **Gap** |
| Monitoring | SOC 2 CC7.2 requires monitoring system components and operations for anomalies, analyzing anomalies to determine whether they are security events, and maintaining detection procedures/tools. | SOP includes Splunk dashboards, DLP/CASB monitoring, PHI read-event review, access-elevation review, bulk-export alerting, and KPI reporting. | **Partial** |
| Vulnerability management | SOC 2 CC7.1 requires configuration standards, monitoring for noncompliance, change-detection mechanisms, detection of unauthorized components, periodic and post-change vulnerability scans, and timely remediation. | SOP mentions a Technical Security Baseline and limited scanning for unencrypted DB connections, but lacks a full vulnerability scanning/remediation process. | **Gap / Partial at best** |

## 1. Change management — does not meet SOC 2 CC8.1

SOC 2 CC8.1 requires an entity to “authorize, design, develop or acquire, configure, document, test, approve, and implement changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures” to meet objectives. SOC 2 CC8.1 also expects controls for tracking changes before implementation, testing changes before production, approving changes before implementation, deploying changes with segregation of responsibilities, and initiating the change process for changes needed to remediate incidents.

SOP-COPS-001 does not establish these required change management controls. It contains:

- A **revision history** for the SOP itself in Section 11.
- An **exception request process** in Section 8.1 requiring ServiceNow submission, risk assessment, approval, expiration, and validation of revocation.
- Some ServiceNow-based access request workflow in Section 5.1.

However, those provisions do **not** amount to a SOC 2 change management process for infrastructure, software, data, configurations, or procedures. Specifically, SOP-COPS-001 is missing:

- Formal change request creation and categorization.
- Required authorization before design/build.
- Documented testing requirements before production.
- Production deployment approval.
- Segregation of duties between developer, tester, and deployer.
- Emergency change handling.
- Rollback/backout procedures.
- Evidence retention requirements.
- Change impact/risk assessment.
- Post-implementation review.
- Linkage between incident remediation and change tickets.

**Conclusion:** SOP-COPS-001 does **not** meet SOC 2 CC8.1 as a standalone change management SOP.

## 2. Monitoring — partially meets SOC 2 CC7.2

SOC 2 CC7.2 requires monitoring of system components and operations for anomalies indicative of malicious acts, natural disasters, and errors, and analysis of those anomalies to determine whether they are security events. SOC 2 CC7.2 points of focus include defined detection policies, tools, logging of unusual system activity, anomaly filtering/analysis, and monitoring detection tools for effective operation.

SOP-COPS-001 has meaningful monitoring controls:

- Section 6.2 requires Netskope CASB to monitor traffic to unmanaged SaaS applications and block/audit restricted-data flows.
- Section 6.2 requires Microsoft Purview DLP to monitor endpoint clipboard operations involving PHI and log incidents in Splunk.
- Section 7 establishes a Splunk compliance dashboard and monthly reporting.
- Section 7.1 defines KPIs for stale access remediation, PHI transmission violations, clean desk adherence, data access request SLA, and unencrypted DB connections.
- Section 7.2 requires weekly review of PHI read events and access elevation events.
- Section 7.2 states that bulk ePHI export events trigger an incident response playbook.

These controls align with parts of SOC 2 CC7.2, especially logging, monitoring, anomaly detection for data access, and escalation of suspicious bulk exports.

However, the SOP is not fully sufficient for SOC 2 CC7.2 because it does not clearly define:

- End-to-end monitoring coverage for all relevant system components, including infrastructure, applications, databases, endpoints, identity systems, cloud services, and network components.
- A governance process for security event detection and management.
- Use of threat intelligence or newly discovered threat/vulnerability feeds.
- Procedures to filter, correlate, and analyze anomalies into security events.
- Requirements to monitor the effectiveness and availability of detection tools.
- Coverage for natural disasters and operational errors, which are expressly included in SOC 2 CC7.2.

**Conclusion:** SOP-COPS-001 **partially meets** SOC 2 CC7.2 for customer-data-focused monitoring, but it is not complete enough to satisfy SOC 2 monitoring expectations across the system.

## 3. Vulnerability management — insufficient for SOC 2 CC7.1 and CC7.4

SOC 2 CC7.1 requires detection and monitoring procedures to identify configuration changes that introduce vulnerabilities and susceptibilities to newly discovered vulnerabilities. SOC 2 CC7.1 expects defined configuration standards, monitoring infrastructure/software for noncompliance, change-detection mechanisms, detection of unknown or unauthorized components, and periodic and post-significant-change vulnerability scans with timely remediation.

SOC 2 CC7.4 also expects incident response procedures to resolve incidents through closure of vulnerabilities and other remediation actions, and to document and communicate remediation activities.

SOP-COPS-001 includes some vulnerability-adjacent controls:

- The definitions section references a “Technical Security Baseline” as the minimum security configuration standard.
- Section 7.1 includes a KPI for “Unencrypted DB Connections,” measured using AWS Trusted Advisor / Nmap internal scan and reported monthly.
- Section 6.2 includes DLP, CASB, MFA, row-level security, and key management controls.
- Section 8.2 escalates suspected breaches to the CIRT and references separate incident response procedures.

But the SOP does not define a vulnerability management program. It is missing:

- Periodic authenticated vulnerability scans for infrastructure and software.
- Scanning after significant changes, as expected by SOC 2 CC7.1.
- Application vulnerability scanning, container/image scanning, dependency scanning, or cloud configuration scanning.
- Severity classification, remediation SLAs, and risk acceptance criteria.
- Ownership for remediation and escalation of overdue vulnerabilities.
- Patch management linkage.
- Verification/retesting after remediation.
- Detection of unauthorized or unknown assets/components.
- File integrity or change-detection mechanisms for critical files/configurations.
- Formal vulnerability exception or risk acceptance process.
- Reporting of vulnerability metrics to management.
- Documentation and communication of remediation activities, as expected by SOC 2 CC7.4.

**Conclusion:** SOP-COPS-001 does **not** meet SOC 2 vulnerability management expectations as a standalone procedure. It contains limited security monitoring and configuration references, but not a full CC7.1/CC7.4 vulnerability management process.

## Overall conclusion

SOP-COPS-001 is primarily a **Customer Data Handling** procedure. It contains useful controls for access, data transmission, DLP, logging, PHI event review, and escalation. Those provisions support portions of SOC 2 security monitoring. However, it is not designed to be a full SOC 2 change management or vulnerability management SOP.

To meet SOC 2 requirements, Meridian should either:

1. Add dedicated sections to SOP-COPS-001 covering change management and vulnerability management, or  
2. Cross-reference separate procedures that fully address SOC 2 CC8.1, CC7.1, CC7.2, CC7.3, and CC7.4, and ensure those procedures are implemented and auditable.

Key missing controls are formal production change approval/testing/deployment controls, vulnerability scanning and remediation SLAs, post-change scanning, asset/configuration monitoring, and defined anomaly analysis procedures.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-DGP-001 (Data Classification and Labeling) compliant with GDPR's transparency and lawful-basis requirements?
question.expected_answer: Partial. Privacy notices exist but lack the Article 13/14 specificity (purposes, lawful basis, retention, rights, recipients, transfers). Lawful basis for processing is mentioned at a policy level but not documented per processing activity (Article 6 / Article 30). The framework is in place but operational documentation is incomplete.

output.answer:
SOP-DGP-001 is **partially compliant** with GDPR transparency and lawful-basis requirements. It contains useful supporting controls, but it does **not** fully satisfy GDPR Articles 12, 13, 14, and 6 as a standalone procedure.

## Assessment

### 1. Transparency requirements: partial support, but incomplete

GDPR requires personal data to be processed “lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner” in relation to the data subject. **GDPR Article 5(1)(a)**. SOP-DGP-001 supports this principle by requiring data classification before or during ingestion and by stating that classification “shall inform the specific privacy notices provided to data subjects.” **SOP-DGP-001 §§ 4.2, 4.4, 5.1**.

However, GDPR transparency is not satisfied merely by internal classification metadata. GDPR requires information to be provided to data subjects in a “concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language.” **GDPR Article 12(1)**. SOP-DGP-001 does not define a procedure for ensuring that privacy notices are clear, accessible, timely, or maintained in sync with classification changes. It only states that Meridian provides “summary privacy notices at point of data collection” and that detailed legal bases are reflected in the classification schema. **SOP-DGP-001 § 4.4**.

For data collected directly from the data subject, GDPR requires disclosure of, among other items, the controller’s identity, DPO contact details, processing purposes and legal basis, legitimate interests where Article 6(1)(f) is used, recipients, international transfers and safeguards, retention period or criteria, data subject rights, and complaint rights. **GDPR Article 13**. SOP-DGP-001 does not require the classification process to verify that each Article 13 notice contains all required elements. **SOP-DGP-001 § 4.4**.

For data not obtained directly from the data subject, GDPR requires similar disclosures plus categories of personal data and source information. **GDPR Article 14**. SOP-DGP-001 covers data assets from third-party feeds and system origins in the inventory process, but it does not require Article 14 notices or source disclosures to data subjects. **SOP-DGP-001 § 5.1 Step 1; GDPR Article 14**.

**Conclusion on transparency:** The SOP provides a useful internal governance mechanism, but it does not fully operationalize GDPR transparency notice obligations.

---

### 2. Lawful basis requirements: partial support, but key gaps

GDPR requires each processing activity to have a lawful basis under Article 6, such as consent, contract necessity, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests. **GDPR Article 6(1)**. SOP-DGP-001 states that the classification engine flags any processing activity that lacks a documented lawful basis in the metadata registry. **SOP-DGP-001 § 4.4**. This is a positive control.

But the SOP does not specify that each processing purpose must be mapped to a specific Article 6 basis, nor does the sample Meridian Standard Label include fields for `processing_purpose`, `article_6_basis`, `legitimate_interests_assessment`, `consent_status`, or `notice_version`. **SOP-DGP-001 § 5.3.1; GDPR Article 6(1)**. The sample label includes `regulatory_regime`, `data_domain`, and indicators such as `ph_indicator`, but it does not demonstrate lawful-basis documentation. **SOP-DGP-001 § 5.3.1**.

GDPR also requires personal data to be collected for “specified, explicit and legitimate purposes” and not further processed incompatibly with those purposes. **GDPR Article 5(1)(b)**. SOP-DGP-001 states that its classification schema enables technical enforcement of purpose limitation for each business unit’s processing activities. **SOP-DGP-001 § 4.4**. However, the detailed labeling standard does not require purpose fields or rules for compatibility assessments when data is reused, reclassified, or used for analytics/model training. **SOP-DGP-001 §§ 5.3, 5.4; GDPR Article 5(1)(b)**.

Because Meridian processes health-related data, the SOP must also account for GDPR special-category data rules. GDPR generally prohibits processing data concerning health unless an Article 9 exception applies, such as explicit consent, medical diagnosis or health care under professional secrecy, public health, or scientific/statistical research with safeguards. **GDPR Article 9(1), 9(2), 9(3)**. SOP-DGP-001 classifies PHI, diagnostic images, patient risk scores, and pseudonymized patient datasets, but it does not require documentation of the applicable Article 9 condition for EU health data. **SOP-DGP-001 §§ 1.2, 5.2.1, 5.2.2; GDPR Article 9**.

**Conclusion on lawful basis:** The SOP recognizes lawful basis as a metadata/control requirement, but it does not sufficiently define the required lawful-basis fields, approval workflow, or Article 9 handling for health data.

---

### 3. Accountability and records: supportive but not complete

GDPR requires the controller to be responsible for and able to demonstrate compliance with the Article 5 principles. **GDPR Article 5(2)**. SOP-DGP-001 has accountability-supporting elements, including a DPO/CPO owner, Data Stewards, Data Custodians, a RACI matrix, annual lifecycle reviews, labeling fidelity metrics, and compliance scorecards. **SOP-DGP-001 §§ 3.1, 3.2, 6.2.1, 7.2, 7.3**.

GDPR also requires records of processing activities to include purposes of processing, categories of data subjects and personal data, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and technical and organizational measures. **GDPR Article 30(1)**. SOP-DGP-001’s CMDB registration requires asset name, system of origin, data domain, data volume, and tentative retention period, but it does not require all Article 30 record fields. **SOP-DGP-001 § 5.1 Step 1; GDPR Article 30(1)**.

**Conclusion on accountability:** The SOP is useful evidence of data governance, but it should be integrated with a GDPR Article 30 record-of-processing procedure.

---

## Required remediation

To reach GDPR compliance for transparency and lawful basis, SOP-DGP-001 should be updated to require:

1. **Mandatory lawful-basis metadata** for every personal-data processing activity, including Article 6 basis, processing purpose, controller/processor role, data subject category, data category, retention basis, and approval owner. **GDPR Articles 5(1)(a), 5(1)(b), 6(1), 30(1)**.

2. **Article 9 condition metadata** for health data and other special-category data, including the specific Article 9 exception and any required safeguards. **GDPR Article 9(1), 9(2), 9(3)**.

3. **Privacy notice linkage**, including notice version, notice URL, language/locale, date provided, and whether the notice satisfies Article 13 or Article 14. **GDPR Articles 12(1), 13, 14**.

4. **A formal review step** requiring the DPO/CPO or Privacy Office to validate that privacy notices include all Article 13/14 elements before a new EU personal-data asset is approved for production processing. **GDPR Articles 13, 14; SOP-DGP-001 §§ 3.2, 5.1**.

5. **Purpose-limitation controls** that prevent reuse of data for analytics, AI training, or secondary purposes unless the lawful basis, Article 9 condition where applicable, and notice coverage are confirmed or updated. **GDPR Articles 5(1)(b), 6(1), 9**.

6. **Article 30 alignment**, so the classification register or CMDB feeds the formal record of processing activities. **GDPR Article 30(1)**.

## Overall conclusion

SOP-DGP-001 is a strong data classification and security-labeling SOP, and it contains some GDPR-supportive language on privacy notices, purpose limitation, minimization, and lawful-basis flagging. **SOP-DGP-001 §§ 4.2, 4.4, 5.1, 5.3, 7.2**. But it does not, by itself, ensure GDPR-compliant transparency notices or lawful-basis documentation at the processing-activity level. **GDPR Articles 12, 13, 14, 6**.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-DGP-005 (Data Access Control and Authorization) satisfy SOC 2 Common Criteria for logical access?
question.expected_answer: Compliant. SOP-DGP-005 establishes role-based access with documented authorisation procedures, periodic review of access rights, segregation of duties for sensitive operations, and timely removal upon role change or termination — aligning with CC6.1 (logical access controls), CC6.2/CC6.3 (registration, authorisation, periodic review), and CC6.7 (information protection during transmission/movement).
question.notes: Ground truth: SOP-DGP-005 × SOC 2 = compliant. Positive control.

output.answer:
Yes — **SOP-DGP-005 substantially satisfies the SOC 2 Common Criteria for logical access at the policy/design level**, especially **CC6.1, CC6.2, and CC6.3**. However, it is **not fully complete for the broader SOC 2 CC6 logical-access-related control set** because it has gaps around architecture security assessment, service account lifecycle detail, boundary protection specificity, and malicious/unauthorized software controls.

## Assessment against SOC 2 logical access criteria

| SOC 2 criterion | SOC 2 requirement | SOP-DGP-005 coverage | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CC6.1 — Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures** | SOC 2 CC6.1 requires the entity to implement logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets. Points of focus include inventorying/classifying assets, assessing new architectures, restricting logical access, identifying/authenticating users, managing credentials for infrastructure/software, encryption, and key protection. | The SOP defines enterprise access controls for PHI, PII, CHD, IP, financial records, credentials, cloud environments, applications, users, and service accounts; requires least privilege, need-to-know, default deny, MFA, RBAC, Okta, IAM, PAM, encryption, and DLP. See SOP §§1.2, 4.1, 5.1, 5.4, 6.1, 6.2. | **Mostly satisfied**, but missing explicit procedure for **security assessment of new architectures before implementation**, which is a SOC 2 CC6.1 point of focus. |
| **CC6.2 — User registration, authorization, credential issuance/removal** | SOC 2 CC6.2 requires that, before issuing credentials and granting access, the entity registers and authorizes new internal and external users, and removes credentials when access is no longer authorized. Points of focus include owner-authorized credential creation, periodic credential validity review, and disabling credentials when invalid. | The SOP has a Joiner-Mover-Leaver process, ServiceNow access request workflow, Data Owner approval, Manager approval, CISO approval for privileged access, immediate Okta suspension on termination, 24-hour deprovisioning, quarterly UAR, and third-party account controls. See SOP §§5.1.1, 5.1.2, 5.1.3, 5.2, 5.3, 5.5. | **Satisfied for human users and third parties. Partial for service accounts**, because service accounts are in scope, but the SOP does not provide a dedicated lifecycle for service account creation, ownership, credential rotation, expiration, and decommissioning. |
| **CC6.3 — Authorize, modify, or remove access based on roles/responsibilities; least privilege and segregation of duties** | SOC 2 CC6.3 requires access to data, software, functions, and protected assets to be authorized, modified, or removed based on roles, responsibilities, system design, and changes, considering least privilege and segregation of duties. Points of focus include owner-authorized access changes, access removal, RBAC, and periodic review of access roles/rules. | The SOP has explicit least privilege, need-to-know, default deny, SoD, manager/Data Owner approval, RBAC, mover recertification, leaver deprovisioning, quarterly UAR, SoD matrix review, and remediation of revoke/modify decisions. See SOP §§4.1, 5.1.2, 5.1.3, 5.2, 5.3, 6.1 ADM-003/ADM-004. | **Satisfied**, assuming the quarterly reviews and remediation timelines are implemented and evidenced. |
| **CC6.6 — Logical access protection from outside system boundaries** | SOC 2 CC6.6 requires logical access security measures to protect against threats from outside system boundaries, including restricted communication channels, protected credentials, additional authentication for external access, and boundary protection systems. | The SOP requires MFA, VPN access, third-party IP allowlisting, SIEM monitoring for third-party accounts, and scoped vendor access. See SOP §§5.1.1, 5.5, 6.2 TEC-001/TEC-002. | **Partially satisfied**. The SOP addresses MFA and third-party restrictions, but does not clearly specify boundary protection controls such as firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR, DMZs, or external access channel restrictions at the level expected by SOC 2 CC6.6. |
| **CC6.7 — Restrict transmission, movement, and removal of information** | SOC 2 CC6.7 requires restricting transmission, movement, and removal of information to authorized users/processes and protecting it during transmission, movement, or removal. Points of focus include DLP, encryption/secure channels, removable media protection, and endpoint protection. | The SOP includes Forcepoint DLP, USB mass-storage blocking, encryption for ePHI at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, endpoint management, third-party monitoring, and SIEM alerting for data exfiltration. See SOP §§4.2, 5.5, 6.2 TEC-006/TEC-007. | **Mostly satisfied**. |
| **CC6.8 — Prevent/detect/act on unauthorized or malicious software** | SOC 2 CC6.8 requires controls to prevent or detect and act on unauthorized or malicious software, including restricting software installation/modification, detecting unauthorized software/configuration changes, defined change control, anti-malware, and scanning externally transferred assets. | SOP-DGP-005 does not include a clear anti-malware, software installation restriction, change-control, file integrity monitoring, or returned-asset malware scanning procedure. | **Gap** for CC6.8 if the SOP is intended to cover the full CC6 logical-access/security control family. This may be covered in another internal policy, but it is not sufficiently addressed in SOP-DGP-005. |

## Key strengths

- **Formal access approval workflow:** SOC 2 CC6.2 requires authorization before credential issuance; the SOP requires ServiceNow requests, business justification, Manager approval, and Data Owner approval before access is granted. See SOC 2 CC6.2; SOP §§5.1.1, 5.2.  
- **Least privilege and RBAC:** SOC 2 CC6.3 expects access based on roles/responsibilities with least privilege and segregation of duties; the SOP explicitly adopts least privilege, need-to-know, default deny, SoD, and RBAC. See SOC 2 CC6.3; SOP §§4.1, 5.1.1, 5.2.  
- **Timely deprovisioning:** SOC 2 CC6.2 and CC6.3 require credentials/access to be removed when no longer authorized or required; the SOP requires immediate Okta suspension on termination and broader deprovisioning within 24 hours. See SOC 2 CC6.2 and CC6.3; SOP §5.1.3.  
- **Periodic access review:** SOC 2 CC6.2 and CC6.3 include periodic review of access credentials, roles, and rules; the SOP requires quarterly UARs, reviewer certification, revoke/modify actions, and remediation within 10 business days. See SOC 2 CC6.2 and CC6.3; SOP §5.3.  
- **Privileged access controls:** SOC 2 CC6.3 expects privileges to be limited and aligned with roles; the SOP requires CISO approval, PAM, JIT access, password injection, session recording, and emergency access alerts. See SOC 2 CC6.3; SOP §5.4.  

## Gaps to remediate

1. **Add architecture security assessment procedure for new systems.**  
   SOC 2 CC6.1 includes assessing new architectures before implementation. SOP-DGP-005 should add a required security review for new IAM architectures, cloud account structures, identity federation, API access patterns, and privileged access designs before production deployment. See SOC 2 CC6.1.

2. **Add a dedicated service account lifecycle.**  
   SOC 2 CC6.2 applies to credentials for individuals, systems, and software; the SOP includes service accounts in scope but does not define service account owner assignment, approval, permitted uses, secret storage, rotation, review frequency, inactivity thresholds, and decommissioning. See SOC 2 CC6.2; SOP §1.2.

3. **Strengthen external boundary access controls.**  
   SOC 2 CC6.6 expects restricted communication channels, additional authentication for external access, and boundary protection systems. The SOP should identify firewall/security group standards, IDS/IPS or EDR coverage, VPN/Zero Trust access rules, prohibited protocols, and monitoring requirements for external access points. See SOC 2 CC6.6.

4. **Address malicious/unauthorized software controls or cross-reference another SOP.**  
   SOC 2 CC6.8 requires controls over software installation/modification, unauthorized configuration changes, anti-malware, change control, and scanning returned/transferred assets. SOP-DGP-005 does not cover these in sufficient detail. If another SOP covers them, SOP-DGP-005 should cross-reference it explicitly. See SOC 2 CC6.8.

5. **Clarify evidence requirements for SOC 2 Type II.**  
   SOC 2 Type II evaluates not only whether controls are suitably designed, but also whether they operated effectively over the audit period. The SOP should identify required evidence artifacts, such as access request tickets, approval records, Okta logs, PAM session logs, UAR completion reports, deprovisioning reports, and exception approvals. See SOC 2 examination description; SOP §§5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 8.1.

## Overall conclusion

SOP-DGP-005 is **well designed for core SOC 2 logical access controls** under **CC6.1, CC6.2, and CC6.3**, particularly for access authorization, provisioning, deprovisioning, RBAC, privileged access, and periodic access review. It should be considered **partially compliant** rather than fully compliant because it does not fully address all relevant SOC 2 CC6 logical-access-related points of focus, especially service account lifecycle detail, new architecture assessment, external boundary protection specificity, and CC6.8 malicious/unauthorized software controls.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-FIN-001 (Credit Scoring Model Governance) meet SR 11-7 expectations for ongoing monitoring and governance?
question.expected_answer: Partial. Ongoing monitoring is referenced but specific performance thresholds and escalation procedures are not defined. Governance structures are described but the roles of model owner vs. independent validator are not delineated — a core SR 11-7 expectation that effective challenge is independent from development. The framework is partially built; effective challenge needs sharpening.

expected_citations.section: Section III (Validation), Section IV (Ongoing Monitoring), Section V (Governance)

output.answer:
Yes—**SOP-FIN-001 substantially addresses SR 11-7 expectations for ongoing monitoring and governance, but it is not fully complete.** I would rate it **partial** because the SOP has strong monitoring cadence, metrics, validation, inventory, escalation, and governance structures, but it leaves several SR 11-7-aligned expectations under-specified: periodic review of model limitations, closed-loop remediation for deteriorating models, business-condition monitoring, stronger independence/effective challenge, and more explicit governance over vendor/third-party models.

## Assessment against SR 11-7

| SR 11-7 expectation | SOP-FIN-001 evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Ongoing monitoring should evaluate whether the model continues to perform as expected given changes in products, exposures, activities, clients, data relevance, or market conditions**. SR 11-7, **V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring**. | SOP §7.1 requires daily automated monitoring for Tier A/B models and weekly Model Health Reports. SOP §7.2 tracks PSI, characteristic drift, default rate by score band, adverse action rate, and human override rate. | **Mostly meets.** Strong technical monitoring exists, but the SOP does not explicitly require monitoring for changes in products, portfolio mix, customer population, underwriting policy, economic/market conditions, or use-case changes. |
| **Monitoring reports’ frequency and scope should depend on model nature, data availability, new approaches, and materiality**. SR 11-7, **V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring**. | SOP §4.4 establishes Tier A/B/C model risk tiers; SOP §7.1 applies daily monitoring to Tier A/B; SOP §7.3 requires annual full re-validation for Tier A and streamlined annual review for Tier B. | **Meets for Tier A/B.** Risk-based cadence is present. However, Tier C monitoring is not described, which may be acceptable only if justified as immaterial. |
| **Monitoring should include procedures for responding to issues before and after approval, including deterioration that may require overlays, adjustment, recalibration, or redevelopment**. SR 11-7, **V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring**. | SOP §8.2 escalates unresolved KPI alerts after 48 hours and again after 5 business days. SOP §5.1.5 permits decommissioning when degradation cannot be remediated. SOP §8.1 requires formal Model Risk Exceptions for deviations. | **Partial.** Escalation exists, but the SOP does not clearly define the remediation workflow for ongoing monitoring breaches—e.g., root-cause analysis, temporary overlays, recalibration, challenger model development, production rollback, validation re-review, approval gates, and closure evidence. |
| **Validation and monitoring should include outcomes analysis comparing model outputs to real-world outcomes, including back-testing or outlier analysis where appropriate**. SR 11-7, **V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Outcomes Analysis**. | SOP §5.1.3 requires validator outcomes analysis, replication on a hold-out “gold set,” back-testing, and sensitivity analysis. SOP §7.2 monitors default rate by score band. SOP §7.3 requires annual re-validation/review. | **Mostly meets.** Initial validation is strong. Ongoing outcomes analysis would be stronger if the SOP specified realized-performance back-testing cadence, required sample-size criteria, vintage/score-band analysis, and thresholds for recalibration. |
| **Governance should be supported by clear policies and effective controls, with expectations and practices commensurate with model risk**. SR 11-7, **VI. Governance and Controls**. | SOP §1 states purpose/scope; SOP §4.4 defines risk tiers; SOP §5 sets lifecycle phases; SOP §7 defines monitoring; SOP §8 defines exceptions and escalation. | **Meets.** The SOP establishes a lifecycle governance framework and applies controls by model materiality. |
| **Roles and responsibilities should be clear, with well-defined accountability and attention to conflicts of interest between development and validation**. SR 11-7, **VI. Governance and Controls — Roles and Responsibilities**. | SOP §3.1 provides a RACI matrix. SOP §3.2 states no member of the development team may validate a model they built. SOP §5.1.3 assigns independent validation. | **Mostly meets, with independence concern.** The validator is independent from development, but SOP §3.2 says the Independent Model Validator reports administratively to the VP of Financial Services, while SOP §3.1 makes that same VP accountable for ongoing monitoring and exception approval. This could weaken perceived independence/effective challenge unless additional safeguards are documented. |
| **Internal audit should evaluate whether model risk management practices are rigorous and effective, rather than duplicating development or validation**. SR 11-7, **VI. Governance and Controls — Roles and Responsibilities**. | SOP §3.1 assigns Internal Audit accountability for external audit and does not assign it development or validation responsibilities. | **Generally meets**, but the SOP should more explicitly state Internal Audit’s periodic review responsibility over the MRM framework and SOP adherence. |
| **An effective model inventory should contain sufficient information to understand model risks at individual and aggregate levels**. SR 11-7, **VI. Governance and Controls — Model Inventory**. | SOP §5.2 requires MRMS inventory fields: Model ID, Owner, Developer, Validation Status, Current Risk Tier, Data Sources, Data Sensitivity Level, and audit log. SOP §5.2 also requires annual owner attestation. | **Mostly meets.** The inventory is strong, but it should add production status, model purpose/intended use, limitations, last validation date, next review date, monitoring status, open findings, dependencies, vendor status, and aggregate risk reporting fields. |
| **Documentation should support tracking recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation efforts**. SR 11-7, **VI. Governance and Controls — Documentation**. | SOP §5.3 requires Model Narrative, Model Risk Exception form, and deployment/rollback authorization. SOP §5.1.3 requires validation reports with findings. SOP §8.1 requires exception documentation and remediation deadlines. | **Mostly meets.** Documentation is robust for validation and exceptions, but ongoing monitoring issue remediation and closure evidence are not fully specified. |
| **For third-party/vendor models, organizations should maintain oversight and integrate vendor work into broader model risk management activities**. SR 11-7, **VI.
