Segregation of duties between developer, approver, and deployer is enforced; emergency-change procedure is documented; rollback procedures exist; change records are retained for audit; communication to affected stakeholders is part of the workflow.
question.notes: Positive case: classic SOC 2 control done well.

output.answer:
Yes—**SOP-ITOP-001 is largely aligned with SOC 2 CC8.1**, but I would rate it **partial rather than fully compliant** because it does not clearly require authorization **before** design/development/acquisition/configuration, and it relies on referenced processes for baseline configuration and patch-change details.

### SOC 2 CC8.1 requirement

SOC 2 CC8.1 requires that the entity “**authorizes, designs, develops or acquires, configures, documents, tests, approves, and implements changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures**” to meet objectives. The CC8.1 points of focus include managing changes across the lifecycle, authorizing changes before design/development/acquisition/configuration, documenting and tracking changes, testing before production, approving changes before implementation, deploying with segregation of responsibilities, initiating changes to remediate incidents, maintaining baseline configurations, emergency change handling, and patch-change management. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1)

### Assessment against CC8.1

| CC8.1 expectation | SOP-ITOP-001 evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Manage changes across the system lifecycle | The SOP covers the lifecycle from RFC submission through post-implementation review and closure. (SOP §§1.2, 5.1) | Meets CC8.1 lifecycle-management intent. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Authorize changes | The SOP prohibits production changes without documented authorization and defines approval workflows for Standard, Normal, Major, Clinical, HealthPay, and Emergency changes. (SOP §§4.3, 5.5.1–5.5.4, 5.7) | Mostly meets approval-before-implementation expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Authorize before design/development/acquisition/configuration | The SOP’s formal approval occurs after impact analysis and planning, and the policy statement focuses on authorization before production implementation. (SOP §§4.3, 5.1, 5.5) | **Gap:** CC8.1 specifically calls for authorization prior to design, development, acquisition, and configuration; the SOP should explicitly add a pre-design/pre-build authorization gate for material system and architecture changes. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Design and develop changes securely | The SOP requires risk-based classification, impact analysis, security/privacy consultation, implementation plans, and special controls for sensitive systems and AI models. (SOP §§4.2, 5.3, 5.5.3, 6.2, 6.3) | Meets the CC8.1 secure-change design/development intent, assuming referenced engineering controls operate as described. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Document changes | RFCs require business justification, affected systems, data sensitivity, maintenance window, implementation plan, test plan, rollback plan, validation steps, and success criteria. (SOP §5.3) | Meets CC8.1 documentation expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Track changes before implementation | Changes are logged in ServiceNow, Standard Changes generate unique RFCs, Normal/Major changes go through CAB workflows, and records are retained in an immutable audit trail. (SOP §§5.3, 5.5.1, 6.2 CM-TEC-05) | Meets CC8.1 tracking expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Configure software and infrastructure in a controlled manner | The SOP covers software, configurations, CI/CD pipelines, IaC templates, COTS applications, infrastructure, cloud resources, and database changes; it requires Terraform for AWS infrastructure changes and prohibits manual production console changes except break-glass. (SOP §§1.2, 6.2 CM-TEC-01, CM-TEC-02, CM-TEC-04) | Meets most CC8.1 configuration-control expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Test changes before production | Normal/Major RFCs require test plans and evidence of staging validation; changes require post-change validation and monitoring. (SOP §§4.5, 5.3, 5.6) | Meets CC8.1 testing-before-production expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Approve changes before implementation | Normal and Major changes require CAB and designated final approvers; Standard Changes are pre-authorized via reviewed templates; Emergency Changes require defined expedited authorization. (SOP §§5.5.1–5.5.4, 5.7) | Meets CC8.1 approval-before-implementation expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Deploy with segregation of responsibilities | The SOP requires segregation between requester, approver, and implementer for Normal/Major changes and includes ServiceNow workflow logic preventing self-approval/self-closure except emergency ratification. (SOP §§3, 4.4, 6.1 CM-ADM-01) | Meets CC8.1 segregation-of-responsibilities deployment expectation. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Identify and evaluate affected objectives | The RFC requires affected systems, data sensitivity, outage expectation, risk score, impact analysis, success criteria, and validation steps. (SOP §§5.2, 5.3) | Meets CC8.1 objective-impact evaluation intent. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Incident-related changes | Emergency Changes are tied to incidents, require a PagerDuty incident ID, expedited approval, documentation, and post-facto CAB/PIR review. (SOP §5.7) | Meets CC8.1 incident-remediation change expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Emergency changes | The SOP defines initiation, authorization, execution, peer/co-pilot expectations, and 72-hour PIR for Emergency Changes. (SOP §5.7) | Meets CC8.1 emergency-change expectations. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Patch changes | The SOP includes routine patching as Standard Changes and actively exploited zero-day patching as Emergency Changes; it also references a separate Vulnerability & Patch Management SOP. (SOP §§5.2, 5.7, 10.1) | Partially addressed in this SOP; full CC8.1 patch-change compliance depends on the referenced patch-management SOP. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |
| Baseline configuration | The SOP requires CMDB updates and references a separate Configuration & Asset Management SOP for CMDB naming and baseline reconciliation. (SOP §§5.6, 7.1, 10.1) | **Partial:** CC8.1 expects baseline configurations to be created and maintained; this SOP references the function but does not itself define baseline creation/maintenance requirements. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1) |

### Key gaps to remediate

1. **Add explicit pre-design/pre-development authorization.**  
   CC8.1 includes authorization before design, development, acquisition, and configuration. The SOP should require a documented authorization or intake approval before material system or architecture work begins, not only before production implementation. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1; SOP §§4.3, 5.1, 5.5)

2. **Define baseline configuration responsibilities directly or incorporate by reference more specifically.**  
   CC8.1 expects baseline configurations of IT technology to be created and maintained. The SOP requires CMDB updates and references SOP-ITOP-005, but it should explicitly state who owns baseline configuration creation, approval, review frequency, and reconciliation evidence. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1; SOP §§5.6, 7.1, 10.1)

3. **Clarify patch-change integration.**  
   CC8.1 includes timely identification, evaluation, testing, approval, and implementation of patches. SOP-ITOP-001 mentions routine patching and emergency patching, but the full patch lifecycle appears to reside in SOP-CISO-002. The SOP should explicitly state that patch changes follow SOP-CISO-002 plus the applicable Standard/Normal/Emergency change workflow. (SOC 2 TSC, CC8.1; SOP §§5.2, 5.7, 10.1)

### Conclusion

SOP-ITOP-001 provides a strong SOC 2 CC8.1 control framework for RFC intake, documentation, risk classification, testing, approval, deployment, emergency changes, audit logging, segregation of duties, validation, and post-implementation review. However, because it does not clearly establish authorization **before** design/development/acquisition/configuration and leaves baseline configuration and patch-change details largely to referenced procedures, it is **not fully compliant on the face of this SOP alone**.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-LEGC-001 (Regulatory Change Management) compliant with the EU AI Act's expectation that providers track and adapt to regulatory developments?
question.expected_answer: Compliant. The EU AI Act doesn't prescribe a specific regulatory-change-tracking process but implies one through Article 17 (quality management system — regulatory compliance strategy), Article 72 (post-market monitoring — adapting to evolving guidance), and Article 78 (confidentiality of regulatory exchanges). SOP-LEGC-001 establishes regulatory-horizon scanning, impact assessment of new requirements, change implementation across affected SOPs, training updates, and reporting to senior management — covering the operational machinery the AI Act expects to exist behind the QMS.
question.notes: Positive case in LEGC BU (previously uncovered).

output.answer:
Yes. SOP-LEGC-001 is compliant with the EU AI Act expectation that providers of high-risk AI systems track and adapt to regulatory developments.

The EU AI Act does not use the exact phrase “track regulatory developments,” but it creates that expectation through the provider’s quality-management, documentation, modification-management, and continuous-compliance duties.

| EU AI Act expectation | Relevant EU AI Act requirement | SOP-LEGC-001 assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Provider must maintain a quality management system that ensures compliance | Providers of high-risk AI systems must have a quality management system compliant with Article 17. EU AI Act, Article 16(c). | The SOP establishes a formal Regulatory Change Management program covering the EU AI Act and requiring identification, assessment, implementation, validation, and monitoring of regulatory obligations. SOP §§1.1, 1.2, 5. |
| QMS must include a regulatory compliance strategy | The QMS must include “a strategy for regulatory compliance,” including conformity assessment and management of modifications to the high-risk AI system. EU AI Act, Article 17(1)(a). | The SOP directly addresses this through horizon scanning, impact assessment, Jira-based remediation planning, control validation, and traceability from regulatory article to operational control. SOP §§4, 5.1–5.4. |
| Provider must manage changes/modifications that may affect compliance | Article 17 requires procedures for management of modifications to high-risk AI systems. EU AI Act, Article 17(1)(a). | The SOP requires regulatory changes to be translated into implementation plans, product/engineering tasks, model-control updates, validation testing, and closure evidence. SOP §§5.2, 5.3, 5.4. |
| Provider must keep technical documentation current | Technical documentation for high-risk AI systems must be “kept up-to date.” EU AI Act, Article 11(1); Article 18 requires providers to maintain such documentation. | The SOP requires auditable traceability in Archer, linked Jira remediation records, verified Alation lineage, and final evidence packages before closure. SOP §§4 POL-LEGC-001-C, 5.4.2. This supports documentation currency, though detailed technical-documentation procedures are cross-referenced to SOP-LEGC-002. SOP §10.1. |
| Provider must evaluate continuous compliance after deployment | Post-market monitoring must actively and systematically collect, document, and analyse data to allow evaluation of continuous compliance with Chapter III, Section 2 requirements. EU AI Act, Article 72(1)–(2). | SOP-LEGC-001 supports continuous compliance at the regulatory-change level through ongoing monitoring, dashboards, KPIs/KRIs, escalation, and quarterly Board reporting. SOP §§7.1–7.3. Product-level post-market monitoring is appropriately referenced to SOP-LEGC-002. SOP §10.1. |
| Provider must be able to demonstrate conformity to authorities | Providers must, upon request, demonstrate conformity of the high-risk AI system with the Chapter III, Section 2 requirements. EU AI Act, Article 16(k). | The SOP creates an audit trail linking source regulation, impact assessment, remediation tasks, lineage, and validation evidence. SOP §§4 POL-LEGC-001-C, 5.4.2, 6.1 AC-05. |

Key strengths:

- The SOP includes the EU AI Act expressly within scope. SOP §1.2.
- It requires daily monitoring of EU official sources, including the Official Journal/EUR-Lex and EC AI Office announcements. SOP §5.1.1.
- It requires regulatory publications to be triaged and logged within two business days. SOP §5.1.2.
- It mandates formal impact assessments for potentially applicable regulatory changes. SOP §4 POL-LEGC-001-D; SOP §5.2.
- It requires remediation plans with owners, timelines, Jira tracking, and escalation for overdue items. SOP §§5.3.1–5.3.2, 8.
- It requires independent validation before closure, which supports EU AI Act quality-management expectations. EU AI Act, Article 17(1); SOP §5.4.1.
- It maintains governance traceability from the specific article or section of regulation to implemented controls. SOP §4 POL-LEGC-001-C.

Minor improvement recommended, but not a compliance gap: the SOP should make explicit that EU AI Act horizon scanning includes harmonised standards, common specifications, implementing acts, and templates issued under the Act, because Article 17 requires the QMS to address technical specifications and standards applied to ensure compliance. EU AI Act, Article 17(1)(e). The SOP likely captures much of this through EUR-Lex, EC AI Office announcements, and standards-body monitoring, but naming “EU AI Act harmonised standards/common specifications/implementing acts” would improve evidentiary clarity. SOP §5.1.1.

Overall, SOP-LEGC-001 satisfies the EU AI Act-derived expectation that a provider maintain a documented, systematic process to monitor regulatory developments, assess their applicability, implement required changes, validate controls, and maintain evidence of continuing compliance.

Compliance level: compliant

question.question: Does SOP-AIML-014 (Model Documentation and Model Cards) satisfy SR 11-7's expectations for model documentation standards?
question.expected_answer: Compliant. SR 11-7 expects model documentation to allow parties unfamiliar with a model to understand its operation, limitations, and key assumptions — including methodology, inputs/outputs, data sources, performance and limitations, and any approved overlays or adjustments. SOP-AIML-014 implements Model Cards capturing intended use, performance metrics across slices, training-data provenance, evaluation methodology, ethical considerations, known limitations, and version history. This documentation supports validation, review by independent parties, and ongoing monitoring — aligning with SR 11-7 Section V.
question.notes: Positive case; boosts SR 11-7 sop_compliance count.

output.answer:
Yes — **SOP-AIML-014 substantially satisfies SR 11-7’s expectations for model documentation standards**, but I would rate it **partial rather than fully compliant** because it does not explicitly require a complete, standalone **independent validation report / validation documentation package** or detailed **implementation-use controls documentation** for each SR 11-7 model.

## Assessment against SR 11-7 documentation expectations

| SR 11-7 expectation | Evidence in SOP-AIML-014 | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Documentation should support effective model risk management, continuity of operations, tracking of recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation. SR 11-7 states that adequate documentation supports continuity, tracking of recommendations/responses/exceptions, and model remediation. **Citation: SR 11-7, Governance and Controls — Documentation.** | SOP requires every production or material-decision model to have an approved Model Card, stored in the Model Inventory System, with version control, audit trails, approvals, exceptions, and archival. **SOP §§4.1, 5.2, 6.3, 6.4, 8.1.** | **Mostly satisfies.** The inventory, audit trail, versioning, and exception process are strong. However, recommendation/response/remediation tracking is not as explicit as SR 11-7 contemplates. |
| Documentation should allow understanding of model purpose, assumptions, methodology, and intended use. SR 11-7 expects development to begin with a clear statement of purpose aligned to intended use and to document design choices, assumptions, methodology, data selection, and developmental testing. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Development, Implementation, and Use; SR 11-7, Model Validation and Monitoring — Conceptual Soundness.** | SOP requires Model Cards to include intended use, target population, architecture, methodology, assumptions, mathematical/statistical methodology, alternative-methodology rationale, expert judgment, and development logs. **SOP §§4.2, 4.3, 5.1.1, 5.1.2.** | **Satisfies.** This is well aligned with SR 11-7 conceptual soundness documentation. |
| Documentation should cover data quality, relevance, inputs, and limitations. SR 11-7 identifies critical assessment of data quality, relevance, and inputs as part of model testing and validation. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Development, Implementation, and Use; SR 11-7, Model Validation and Monitoring — Conceptual Soundness.** | SOP requires documentation of data source provenance, descriptive statistics, missing/extreme values, preprocessing, representativeness, proxy variables, data quality certificate, and known limitations/biases. **SOP §§4.2, 4.3, 5.1.1, 5.1.3.** | **Satisfies.** The SOP is specific and operational. |
| Documentation should address testing, benchmarking, sensitivity, back-testing, and outcomes analysis where appropriate. SR 11-7 describes testing such as out-of-sample/out-of-time testing, comparison of alternative assumptions/methodologies, benchmarking, back-testing, outlier analysis, and outcomes analysis. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Development, Implementation, and Use; SR 11-7, Model Validation and Monitoring — Outcomes Analysis.** | SOP requires independent test set performance, ROC-AUC, calibration, subgroup performance, KS statistic, sensitivity/stability testing, benchmarking, back-testing where available, stress testing, and ongoing outcomes-analysis metrics and thresholds. **SOP §§4.2, 5.1.3, 5.4, 7.1.** | **Satisfies.** Testing documentation is comprehensive. |
| Users should understand model limitations and avoid use beyond intended purpose. SR 11-7 states that effective model use depends on a clear understanding of limitations and that use beyond intended purpose introduces additional uncertainty and risk. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Development, Implementation, and Use — Model Use.** | SOP requires Model Cards to specify intended use, target population, decision context, known limitations, out-of-scope uses, and annual limitations review based on operational incidents. **SOP §§4.3, 5.4.** | **Satisfies.** This is a strong control. |
| Ongoing monitoring should evaluate whether the model continues to perform as expected, and monitoring frequency should reflect model nature, data availability, and materiality. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring.** | SOP requires monitoring plans, monthly monitoring for Critical Models, quarterly monitoring for other SR 11-7 Models, annual recertification, drift thresholds, escalation, and remediation timelines. **SOP §§4.2, 4.4, 5.4, 7.1, 7.3.** | **Satisfies.** Monitoring documentation is well defined. |
| Governance should include clear roles, responsibilities, accountability, and independence between model development and validation. **Citation: SR 11-7, Governance and Controls — Roles and Responsibilities.** | SOP includes RACI, named accountability, Model Owner, independent Model Validator, CCO oversight, segregation of duties, and independent validator approval for SR 11-7 Model Cards. **SOP §§3, 5.1.4, 6.2.** | **Satisfies.** Independence expectations are addressed. |
| A model inventory should contain sufficient information to understand model risks at individual and aggregate levels. **Citation: SR 11-7, Governance and Controls — Model Inventory.** | SOP establishes a Model Inventory System with model IDs, version-controlled Model Cards, metadata, SR 11-7 status, risk classification, review due dates, audit trail, and production reconciliation. **SOP §§5.2, 7.1, 7.3.** | **Satisfies.** Strong alignment. |
| Vendor and third-party model documentation should be subject to oversight and integrated into broader model risk management. **Citation: SR 11-7, Vendor and Other Third-Party Products.** | SOP requires vendor documentation equivalent to a Meridian Model Card, internal Model Card creation, Meridian-led evaluation, gap analysis, annual performance reports for SR 11-7 vendor models, and formal risk acceptance where gaps remain. **SOP §5.5.** | **Satisfies.** This is appropriately tailored for vendor models. |

## Key gaps / enhancements needed

1. **Independent validation documentation is not fully specified.**  
   SR 11-7 expects validation to assess reliability, limitations, assumptions, methods, data, and relevant theories, and to identify limitations, errors, and corrective actions. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Validation and Monitoring.** SOP-AIML-014 requires a Senior Model Validator review and references validation reports in archiving, but it does not define a required **validation report template** or required contents such as validation scope, tests performed, challenger/benchmark results, findings, severity ratings, limitations, unresolved issues, management responses, and remediation tracking. **SOP §§5.1.4, 5.6.**

2. **Recommendation / finding / remediation tracking should be more explicit.**  
   SR 11-7 says documentation supports tracking recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation. **Citation: SR 11-7, Governance and Controls — Documentation.** SOP-AIML-014 has exception tracking and deficiency resubmission, but it should explicitly require a controlled log for validator findings, management responses, due dates, owners, remediation status, and closure evidence. **SOP §§6.3, 8.1.**

3. **Implementation and model-use documentation could be strengthened.**  
   SR 11-7 covers the full lifecycle, including model development, implementation, and use, and notes that effective use depends on understanding limitations and intended use. **Citation: SR 11-7, Model Development, Implementation, and Use — Model Use.** SOP-AIML-014 covers intended use and code repository references, but the Model Card minimum content does not clearly require production implementation details such as deployment architecture, input/output controls, user procedures, override procedures, access/entitlement controls for model use, production reconciliation, and implementation testing. **SOP §§4.3, 5.1.2, 5.1.3.**

## Bottom line

SOP-AIML-014 is **well designed and largely aligned** with SR 11-7’s model documentation expectations. It covers model purpose, methodology, assumptions, data, testing, limitations, monitoring, governance, inventory, versioning, vendor models, and independence. However, to fully satisfy SR 11-7 documentation standards, Meridian should add explicit requirements for:  
- a formal independent validation report package,  
- tracking of validation findings, responses, and remediation, and  
- production implementation/use-control documentation.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-FIN-010 (Regulatory Capital Reporting) compliant with SR 11-7's expectations for the models used in regulatory capital calculations?
question.expected_answer: Compliant. SOP-FIN-010 treats regulatory capital models as Tier 1 / high-materiality under its risk-tiering framework: independent validation prior to use and on a defined recurring cadence, ongoing performance monitoring with documented thresholds, comprehensive model documentation (assumptions, methodology, limitations, sensitivity), defined model owners and independent validators, and escalation procedures to senior management and the board. The SOP covers the three foundational elements of SR 11-7: robust development/implementation, sound validation with effective challenge, and governance/policies/controls.
question.notes: Positive case; SR 11-7 boost in Financial Services BU.

output.answer:
SOP-FIN-010 is **substantially aligned but not fully compliant** with SR 11-7 expectations for models used in regulatory capital calculations. It has strong coverage of governance, monitoring, documentation, independence, data controls, and escalation, but it has a few material gaps around **explicit pre-use/material-change validation**, **validation content requirements**, and **vendor/third-party model product oversight**.

## Assessment against SR 11-7

| SR 11-7 expectation | SOP-FIN-010 evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Models in scope should be governed according to their risk and use.** SR 11-7 defines models as quantitative methods/systems applying statistical, economic, or financial theories to produce estimates, and expects model risk management to be tailored to model risk, purpose, and use. *(SR 11-7, Applicability/Scope; §III Overview of Model Risk and Model Risk Management)* | SOP identifies PD, LGD, EAD, retail PD/LGD, macroeconomic scenario, and RWA/capital calculation components used for A-IRB regulatory capital. It classifies all regulatory capital models as Tier 1. *(SOP §§1.2, 2.1, 5.3, 6.2)* | **Compliant.** The core capital risk-parameter models are clearly in scope and treated as high materiality. |
| **Model development should be aligned with purpose, intended use, data, methodology, assumptions, and testing.** SR 11-7 expects development to begin with clear purpose and include testing such as out-of-sample/out-of-time testing, alternative assumptions/methodologies, and data-quality assessment, commensurate with model materiality. *(SR 11-7 §IV, Model Development)* | SOP requires model documentation covering theoretical basis, mathematical specifications, data sources, data-quality assessment, development sample, methodology, estimation results, limitations, use constraints, and code. It also defines data-quality controls and model execution procedures. *(SOP §§4.2 RC-P-003, 5.2, 5.3, 6.2 Ctl-020–Ctl-025)* | **Mostly compliant.** Documentation and data-quality expectations are strong, but the SOP does not explicitly require development-stage testing such as out-of-time/out-of-sample testing or challenger/alternative methodology assessment for every new or materially changed model. |
| **Model use should reflect model limitations and intended purpose.** SR 11-7 states effective model use depends on a clear understanding of limitations, and use beyond intended purpose requires additional analysis and controls. *(SR 11-7 §IV, Model Use)* | SOP requires model limitations to be disclosed in technical reference documents and in every submission of model output to management or the Board. It also requires management review of assumptions, limitations, overlays, and open findings. *(SOP §§6.2 Ctl-025, 5.6)* | **Compliant.** Limitations and management challenge are embedded in the reporting process. |
| **Validation should evaluate conceptual soundness, reliability, limitations, errors, and appropriate use.** SR 11-7 expects validation to assess whether models perform as expected, identify limitations/errors, and evaluate reliability based on assumptions, methods, data, and relevant theory. *(SR 11-7 §V, Model Validation and Monitoring; §V, Conceptual Soundness)* | SOP requires annual independent validation by MRM and biennial external validation for A-IRB models. It also requires independent replication during each reporting cycle. *(SOP §§4.2 RC-P-001/RC-P-002, 5.5, 6.2 Ctl-010–Ctl-012)* | **Partial.** The annual validation requirement exists, but the SOP’s annual Model Validation Report control mainly specifies risk rating and model-use continuation. It does not explicitly state that each validation must cover conceptual soundness, assumptions, methodology, developmental evidence, implementation/code review, limitations, outcomes analysis, and appropriate-use conclusions. |
| **Validation should generally occur before first use; if used before validation, limitations and controls should be heightened.** SR 11-7 states validation generally occurs before first use, and if urgent use occurs before validation, limitations must be communicated and controls strengthened. *(SR 11-7 §V, Model Validation and Monitoring)* | SOP requires annual validation and MRM validation sign-off for MLflow promotion, and annual recalibration must be validated before Q1 calculation. *(SOP §§6.3 MLflow Model Registry, 6.2 Ctl-060)* | **Partial / gap.** The SOP does not clearly require **full independent validation before initial production use** or before **all material model changes** for regulatory capital models, nor does it define temporary-use controls if validation is incomplete. This is a material SR 11-7 gap for capital models. |
| **Ongoing monitoring should evaluate performance as products, exposures, clients, data, and market conditions change.** SR 11-7 expects ongoing monitoring, periodic review of limitations, and responses such as overlays, adjustment, recalibration, or redevelopment when models no longer perform as expected. *(SR 11-7 §V, Ongoing Model Monitoring)* | SOP requires quarterly monitoring with outcome analysis, PD back-testing, LGD recovery assessment, PSI, AUROC/AR, Hosmer-Lemeshow, K-S, supervisory floor comparisons, red/amber/green thresholds, breach investigation, fitness-for-purpose assessment, recalibration/redevelopment, and quarterly Model Risk Committee reporting. *(SOP §§4.2 RC-P-004/RC-P-005, 5.10, 6.2 Ctl-030–Ctl-038, 6.4, 7.1)* | **Compliant.** Monitoring is detailed, quantitative, threshold-based, and linked to escalation and remediation. |
| **Outcomes analysis/back-testing should compare model outputs to real-world outcomes and support corrective action.** SR 11-7 identifies outcomes analysis, back-testing, and outlier analysis as sound validation/monitoring practices. *(SR 11-7 §V, Outcomes Analysis)* | SOP requires PD back-testing against realized defaults, LGD comparison to recovery realizations, EAD/RWA replication, outlier review for large RWA contributors, and impact assessment of breaches on RWA and capital ratios. *(SOP §§4.2 RC-P-004, 5.3, 5.5, 5.10, 6.4)* | **Compliant.** The SOP includes strong outcomes-analysis controls for regulatory capital use. |
| **Governance should include policies, controls, roles, responsibilities, accountability, and conflict management.** SR 11-7 expects model governance supported by clear policies, effective controls, and defined accountability across development, validation, and monitoring. *(SR 11-7 §VI, Governance and Controls; §VI, Roles and Responsibilities)* | SOP includes a RACI, named accountable owners, three lines of defense, Model Risk Committee reporting, Board escalation, exception handling, issue tracking, and management attestation. *(SOP §§3, 4, 6.1, 6.2, 7.3, 8)* | **Compliant.** Governance is well defined and proportionate to Tier 1 regulatory capital model risk. |
| **Effective challenge should be performed by objective experts with independence, expertise, and authority.** SR 11-7 expects effective challenge across the lifecycle by sufficiently independent and expert reviewers. *(SR 11-7 §III Overview; §VI Roles and Responsibilities)* | SOP separates MRM from model development, restricts validators from having developed the same model in the prior 36 months, assigns MRM validation ownership, and requires CRO/Head of MRM challenge during management review. *(SOP §§3.3, 5.5, 5.6, 6.2)* | **Compliant.** Independence and challenge mechanisms are present. |
| **Model inventory and documentation should support aggregate and individual model risk management.** SR 11-7 states effective inventories contain sufficient information to understand model risks; documentation supports continuity, remediation, recommendations, responses, and exceptions. *(SR 11-7 §VI, Model Inventory; §VI, Documentation)* | SOP requires model inventory records with model ID, owner, tier, validation date, monitoring date, and status; documentation repositories; code repositories; data lineage; limitations; and retention of execution logs. *(SOP §§5.3, 6.2 Ctl-020–Ctl-025, 6.3)* | **Compliant.** Inventory and documentation controls are robust. |
| **Internal audit should assess the effectiveness of model risk management rather than duplicate validation.** SR 11-7 states internal audit generally evaluates whether model risk management practices are rigorous and effectively implemented, rather than duplicating development or validation. *(SR 11-7 §VI, Roles and Responsibilities)* | SOP assigns Internal Audit to annual independent review of the regulatory capital reporting control environment, testing submissions and reporting to the Board Audit Committee. *(SOP §§6.1, 7.4)* | **Compliant.** Internal Audit’s role is appropriately positioned as third-line assurance. |
| **Vendor and third-party products require oversight, understanding, validation, documentation, ongoing monitoring, and evaluation of customizations.** SR 11-7 states vendor models/products remain subject to model risk management, including understanding conceptual soundness, design, development data, performance, ongoing monitoring, and documentation of customizations. *(SR 11-7 §VII, Vendor and Other Third-Party Products)* | SOP identifies SAS as the regulatory capital engine provider, KPMG as external validator, Deloitte as auditor, and OneSumX for EU filings, but it does not define a vendor model/product validation or due-diligence process for SAS/OneSumX components or any proprietary third-party model components. *(SOP §§1.3, 5.7, 5.9, 6.3)* | **Gap.** The SOP needs explicit third-party/vendor product governance for capital-related vendor components, including validation approach, access limitations, vendor documentation review, customization assessment, and ongoing performance monitoring. |

## Key gaps to remediate

1. **Add explicit pre-use and material-change validation requirements.**  
   SR 11-7 expects validation generally before first use, with heightened controls if a model is used before validation is complete. *(SR 11-7 §V, Model Validation and Monitoring)* SOP-FIN-010 should require full independent MRM validation before initial production use and before any material change to PD, LGD, EAD, macro, or other capital models, with defined temporary-use restrictions if validation is incomplete.

2. **Define the required content of annual validation reports.**  
   SR 11-7 expects validation to assess conceptual soundness, reliability, limitations, errors, appropriate use, outcomes analysis, and ongoing monitoring results. *(SR 11-7 §V, Model Validation and Monitoring; §V, Conceptual Soundness; §V, Outcomes Analysis)* SOP-FIN-010 should require each MVR to cover, at minimum: conceptual soundness, data suitability, assumptions, methodology, developmental evidence, implementation/code verification, benchmarking/challenger review where appropriate, outcomes analysis, limitations, use restrictions, and remediation findings.

3. **Strengthen development-stage testing requirements.**  
   SR 11-7 identifies testing such as out-of-sample/out-of-time testing, alternative assumptions/methodologies, and data-quality assessment as sound development practice. *(SR 11-7 §IV, Model Development)* SOP-FIN-010 should explicitly require those tests for new models and material redevelopments, especially because A-IRB capital models are Tier 1.

4. **Add third-party/vendor model product governance.**  
   SR 11-7 applies model risk principles to vendor and third-party products, including understanding conceptual soundness, design, data, performance, ongoing monitoring, and customization impacts. *(SR 11-7 §VII, Vendor and Other Third-Party Products)* SOP-FIN-010 should add controls for SAS Regulatory Capital Engine, OneSumX, and any vendor-supplied parameters/components used in capital reporting.

## Bottom line

SOP-FIN-010 is **well designed for ongoing SR 11-7-style model risk management of regulatory capital models**, especially monitoring, governance, documentation, issue escalation, and management attestation. However, because it lacks explicit requirements for **pre-use/material-change validation**, **minimum validation report scope**, and **vendor product oversight**, it should be rated **partially compliant**, not fully compliant.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-CLIN-006 (Clinical AI Human Override Procedures) compliant with HIPAA's expectations for ePHI access control and audit when clinicians override AI outputs?
question.expected_answer: Compliant. The SOP implements role-based access control to ePHI (§164.312(a)) for the clinician override interface, automatic audit logging of every override event including user, timestamp, reason code, and PHI accessed (§164.312(b) audit controls), and integrity controls (§164.312(c)) so that override actions cannot alter the underlying ePHI record. The override procedure preserves the minimum-necessary standard (§164.502(b)) and ties into the workforce-training programme (§164.308(a)(5)). PHI accessed via override pathways is included in accounting-of-disclosures procedures where required.

output.answer:
SOP-CLIN-006 is **partially compliant** with HIPAA’s ePHI access-control and audit expectations for clinician overrides of AI outputs. It has strong override-specific controls, but it does not fully evidence all HIPAA-required or expected access-control and audit-review procedures within the SOP itself.

| HIPAA expectation | HIPAA citation | SOP-CLIN-006 assessment |
|---|---:|---|
| Limit ePHI access to authorized persons/software programs. | 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(1), tied to access rights under § 164.308(a)(4) | **Mostly met.** SOP § 6.1 restricts Accept/Modify/Reject to `ClarityClinicalUser` via RBAC, with role assignment through IAM integrated with customer AD/LDAP. |
| Unique user identification. | 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(i) | **Met.** SOP § 6.2 maps unique user identification to LDAP/SAML and logs User ID for override events. |
| Person/entity authentication. | 45 CFR § 164.312(d) | **Met.** SOP § 6.1 requires MFA for production access; SOP § 5.2 assumes authenticated EHR SSO/SMART-on-FHIR context. |
| Automatic logoff. | 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(iii) | **Met as documented.** SOP § 6.2 states automatic logoff after 15 minutes of inactivity. |
| Encryption/decryption and transmission security for ePHI. | 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(iv), § 164.312(e)(1)-(2) | **Mostly met, with ambiguity.** SOP § 6.2 states TLS 1.3/HTTPS for PHI in transit. However, SOP § 8.1 stores in-progress override content in local browser storage for 24 hours without specifying encryption, access restriction, or secure purge. SMS/email urgent notifications in SOP § 5.6 also need clearer PHI-minimization and transmission controls. |
| Audit controls for systems containing or using ePHI. | 45 CFR § 164.312(b) | **Strongly met for override events.** SOP § 6.2 logs dialog opened, Modify Saved, Reject Saved, Explicit Accept, Tacit Accept, post-hoc rationale edits, timestamps, User ID, Patient ID, Session ID, model/recommendation IDs, pre/post state, rationale, and success/failure. |
| Integrity protections for ePHI and audit records. | 45 CFR § 164.312(c)(1)-(2) | **Met for audit trail.** SOP § 6.1 requires cryptographic signing, immutability, no deletion API, and append-only corrections; SOP § 6.2 maps integrity to cryptographic signing and message authentication codes. |
| Regular review of information system activity, including audit logs and access reports. | 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(D) | **Partially met.** SOP § 3 assigns monthly audit-trail integrity verification, and SOP § 7 monitors override patterns. But it does not clearly require HIPAA-style security/privacy review of override audit logs for inappropriate access, anomalous access, failed access attempts, privilege misuse, or export activity. |
| Workforce access authorization, clearance, modification, and termination procedures. | 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(3), § 164.308(a)(4)(B)-(C) | **Partially met by reference.** SOP § 6.1 says role assignment is managed through IAM/AD/LDAP and SOP § 9 links training completion to provisioning/suspension. But the SOP does not itself define access approval criteria, periodic access review, role modification, or termination/deprovisioning steps. |
| Emergency access procedure. | 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(ii) | **Gap.** HIPAA requires procedures for obtaining necessary ePHI during an emergency. SOP § 8.1 addresses platform unavailability and interim EHR documentation, but it does not define an emergency/break-glass access procedure for necessary ePHI or override functions. |
| Documentation retention. | 45 CFR § 164.316(b)(1)-(2) | **Met for audit logs.** SOP § 6.2 retains override audit records for at least 6 years, consistent with HIPAA’s 6-year documentation retention requirement. |

**Key strengths**

- The SOP captures override-specific audit evidence at a granular level: clinician identity, patient ID, session ID, action, model/recommendation ID, pre/post state, rationale, and success/failure. This aligns well with HIPAA’s audit-control requirement to record activity in systems containing or using ePHI under 45 CFR § 164.312(b).
- Access to override functionality is role-restricted and authenticated through RBAC, IAM, SAML/LDAP, and MFA, supporting HIPAA access control and authentication under 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(1), § 164.312(a)(2)(i), and § 164.312(d).
- The audit trail is immutable and cryptographically protected, supporting HIPAA integrity expectations under 45 CFR § 164.312(c).
- The SOP includes 6-year retention for override audit records, consistent with 45 CFR § 164.316(b)(2)(i).

**Gaps to remediate**

1. **Add emergency access / break-glass procedures.**  
   HIPAA requires emergency access procedures under 45 CFR § 164.312(a)(2)(ii). SOP § 8.1 addresses downtime, but not emergency access to necessary ePHI or override functionality.

2. **Define formal access lifecycle controls.**  
   Add or cross-reference specific procedures for access approval, role changes, periodic access recertification, and termination/deprovisioning to satisfy 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(3) and § 164.308(a)(4)(B)-(C).

3. **Strengthen audit review requirements.**  
   SOP § 7 reviews clinical override metrics, but HIPAA also expects regular review of information system activity such as audit logs, access reports, and security incident reports under 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(D). Add a privacy/security audit-log review cadence, reviewer role, exception criteria, and escalation path.

4. **Secure local browser storage used during failed override saves.**  
   SOP § 8.1 allows in-progress override content to remain in local browser storage for 24 hours. If that content contains ePHI, the SOP should require encryption, device/session binding, automatic purge, and controls against access by other users, consistent with 45 CFR § 164.312(a), § 164.312(c), and § 164.312(e).

5. **Clarify SMS/email notification content.**  
   SOP § 5.6 should explicitly state that SMS/email notifications contain no unencrypted ePHI, or document encryption/secure messaging safeguards, consistent with HIPAA transmission security under 45 CFR § 164.312(e)(1)-(2).

Overall, SOP-CLIN-006 is well designed for **override-event auditability** and **role-based clinician access**, but it is not fully complete as a HIPAA access-control and audit procedure because emergency access, access lifecycle governance, HIPAA security audit review, and local-storage/notification safeguards need clearer treatment.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-AIML-007 (Model Retraining and Continuous Learning) align with NIST AI RMF's MEASURE and MANAGE functions for handling distributional drift and emergent risks?
question.expected_answer: Compliant. SOP-AIML-007 implements continuous monitoring of distributional drift, performance decay, and fairness/bias metrics with defined thresholds (MEASURE 2/3/4), tied to retraining triggers and approval gates (MANAGE 1/2/3). Retraining requires re-validation against pre-defined acceptance criteria, documented retraining-data provenance, and re-issuance of model cards. Stakeholder feedback channels (clinical/business teams) close the loop into MAP for emergent-risk identification. Risk tolerances from GOVERN are referenced for prioritisation.
question.notes: Positive case; NIST AI RMF boost.

output.answer:
Yes — **SOP-AIML-007 substantially aligns with NIST AI RMF MEASURE and MANAGE for distributional drift**, but it is **only partially aligned for emergent/previously unknown risks** because it lacks a complete feedback, appeal/override, and emergent-risk triage loop tied to affected users and communities.

## Summary assessment

| Area | Alignment | Rationale |
|---|---:|---|
| Distributional drift detection and measurement | Strong | SOP defines data, concept, label, and prediction-distribution drift; sets quantitative thresholds; monitors production logs continuously; and uses drift-triggered retraining. This aligns with NIST AI RMF MEASURE’s expectation that organizations identify and apply appropriate methods and metrics for AI risk measurement. |
| Validation after drift-triggered retraining | Strong | SOP requires performance, robustness, fairness, shadow deployment, and independent model validation before promotion, aligning with NIST AI RMF MEASURE requirements for documented test sets, metrics, TEVV tools, and independent assessment. |
| Drift response and recovery | Strong | SOP defines retraining tickets, priority levels, promotion gates, canary deployment, rollback triggers, RCA, and post-deployment monitoring, aligning with NIST AI RMF MANAGE requirements for post-deployment monitoring, response, recovery, change management, and deactivation/supersession mechanisms. |
| Emergent risks / previously unknown risks | Partial | SOP includes manual triggers for adverse events, regulatory changes, audit findings, and security vulnerabilities, plus escalation and rollback. However, it does not fully implement NIST AI RMF expectations for feedback from end users/impacted communities, appeal/override mechanisms, and risk tracking where risks are hard to measure. |

## MEASURE function alignment

### 1. Appropriate methods and metrics are identified and applied

SOP-AIML-007 aligns well with **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.1**, which calls for selecting approaches and metrics for measuring AI risks, starting with the most significant risks. The SOP defines drift-specific metrics and trigger thresholds, including PSI > 0.25, KS p-value < 0.01, AUC/PR-AUC degradation, label drift, and KL divergence for prediction distribution shift. This is directly responsive to distributional drift measurement.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 1.1; SOP-AIML-007 §5.1.2.

The SOP also supports **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.2**, which requires regular assessment and updating of metrics and controls, by requiring continuous monitoring, KPI tracking for drift detection and response, quarterly AI Governance reporting, and revision history showing periodic refinement of drift thresholds.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 1.2; SOP-AIML-007 §§7.1, 7.3, 11.

### 2. Independent and contextual assessment

The SOP aligns with **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.3**, which expects internal experts or independent assessors to be involved in regular assessments. SOP-AIML-007 assigns Model Validation responsibility for validation testing, requires independence from development teams, and uses approval gates before production promotion.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 1.3; SOP-AIML-007 §§3, 5.5, 6.2.3.

However, alignment is weaker where **MEASURE 1.3** and **MEASURE 4.1–4.3** call for consultation with domain experts, users, relevant AI actors, and affected communities where appropriate. The SOP includes internal domain roles such as Clinical AI Safety Officer and Financial Model Risk Officer, but it does not establish a defined process for incorporating end-user or affected-community feedback into drift or emergent-risk measurement.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 1.3, MEASURE 4.1–4.3; SOP-AIML-007 §§3, 5.5, 7.3.

### 3. Test sets, metrics, and TEVV documentation

The SOP strongly aligns with **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.1**, which requires documentation of test sets, metrics, and TEVV tools. SOP-AIML-007 specifies temporal holdout datasets, metric thresholds, robustness tests, fairness metrics, shadow deployment requirements, and Model Validation Reports.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 2.1; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.1.3, 5.4, 5.5.

The SOP also supports **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.3**, which requires performance or assurance criteria to be measured for conditions similar to deployment context. SOP-AIML-007 uses production inference logs, recent temporal holdout data, shadow deployments with 100% production traffic for Tier 1 and Tier 2 models, and post-deployment monitoring.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 2.3; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.1.2, 5.1.3, 5.4.4, 5.6.

### 4. Risk tracking where measurement is difficult

A gap exists against **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 3.2**, which expects risk tracking approaches for settings where AI risks are difficult to assess or where metrics are not yet available. SOP-AIML-007 is strong for measurable drift and performance degradation, but it does not define a dedicated tracking method for novel, ambiguous, or non-quantifiable emergent risks that may not be captured by predefined statistical thresholds.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 3.2; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.1.2, 5.1.4, 8.3.

### 5. Feedback, problem reporting, and appeals

A material gap exists against **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 3.3**, which calls for feedback processes for end users and impacted communities to report problems and appeal system outcomes, integrated into evaluation metrics. SOP-AIML-007 has internal manual triggers and adverse-event escalation, but it does not define user-facing feedback intake, appeal/override capture, or integration of those signals into drift or retraining metrics.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 3.3; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.1.4, 8.3.

## MANAGE function alignment

### 1. Sustaining value of deployed AI systems

SOP-AIML-007 aligns with **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 2.2**, which requires mechanisms to sustain the value of deployed AI systems. The SOP requires scheduled retraining, data freshness windows, continuous monitoring, retraining triggers, validation gates, and post-deployment monitoring.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 2.2; SOP-AIML-007 §§4.1, 5.1, 5.2.3, 5.6, 7.1.

### 2. Responding to previously unknown risks

The SOP partially aligns with **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 2.3**, which requires procedures to respond to and recover from a previously unknown risk when identified. SOP-AIML-007 includes manual retraining triggers for adverse event investigations, audit findings, regulatory or policy changes, and security vulnerabilities, and it includes rollback and incident response processes.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 2.3; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.1.4, 5.7, 8.3.

The gap is that the SOP does not define an explicit “emergent risk” workflow covering intake, triage, severity scoring, risk owner assignment, interim controls, stakeholder communication, and post-risk review for unknown risks that do not immediately present as drift, performance degradation, adverse clinical event, or security vulnerability.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 2.3, MANAGE 4.1; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.1.4, 5.7, 8.

### 3. Superseding, disengaging, or deactivating models

The SOP aligns strongly with **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 2.4**, which requires mechanisms and assigned responsibilities to supersede, disengage, or deactivate AI systems that show performance or outcomes inconsistent with intended use. SOP-AIML-007 requires rollback targets, rollback triggers, rollback execution timelines, quarantine of failed versions, and RCA.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 2.4; SOP-AIML-007 §§4.1, 5.7.

### 4. Post-deployment monitoring, incident response, recovery, and change management

The SOP aligns with **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 4.1**, which requires post-deployment monitoring plans including mechanisms for incident response, recovery, and change management. SOP-AIML-007 includes model health dashboards, retraining pipeline dashboards, regulatory compliance dashboards, canary deployment, rollback, incident ticketing, RCA, and escalation paths.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 4.1; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.6, 5.7, 7.2, 8.3.

The SOP is weaker against the parts of **MANAGE 4.1** requiring mechanisms for capturing and evaluating input from users and relevant AI actors, appeal and override, and decommissioning. SOP-AIML-007 references rollback and related SOP-AIML-011 for decommissioning, but it does not itself define user feedback, appeal, or override handling for retraining decisions or model outputs.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 4.1; SOP-AIML-007 §§5.7, 10.1.

### 5. Continual improvement

The SOP aligns with **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 4.2**, which requires measurable continual improvement activities integrated into system updates. SOP-AIML-007 includes KPIs for MTTD, MTTR, freshness compliance, validation success rate, rollback rate, shadow pass rate, fairness compliance, and documentation completeness. It also requires quarterly AI Governance reporting and NIST AI RMF alignment review.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 4.2; SOP-AIML-007 §§7.1, 7.3.

However, the SOP does not fully satisfy the stakeholder-engagement aspect of **MANAGE 4.2**, because continual improvement is primarily driven by internal operational and validation metrics rather than structured engagement with users, affected communities, or external AI actors.  
**Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 4.2; SOP-AIML-007 §§7.1, 7.3.

## Key gaps to close

1. **Add an emergent-risk intake and triage workflow.**  
   Define a process for identifying risks not captured by drift/performance thresholds, including severity scoring, ownership, interim mitigations, timelines, and escalation. This would strengthen alignment with **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 2.3** and **MANAGE 4.1**.  
   **Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 2.3, MANAGE 4.1.

2. **Integrate user and affected-community feedback into measurement.**  
   Add channels for clinicians, financial-services users, patients/customers where appropriate, and impacted communities to report unexpected behavior, harms, or contested outcomes. Feed these signals into model evaluation metrics and retraining triggers. This would address **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 3.3** and **MEASURE 4.1–4.3**.  
   **Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 3.3, MEASURE 4.1–4.3.

3. **Define appeal and override capture.**  
   For models affecting clinical, credit, claims, or other consequential decisions, document how overrides and appeals are recorded, analyzed, and used to detect emergent risks. This is specifically contemplated by **NIST AI RMF MANAGE 4.1** and **MEASURE 3.3**.  
   **Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MANAGE 4.1, MEASURE 3.3.

4. **Track hard-to-measure risks.**  
   Add a risk register category for qualitative, uncertain, or novel risks where quantitative metrics are not yet available, with periodic review and documentation. This would improve alignment with **NIST AI RMF MEASURE 3.2**.  
   **Citations:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, MEASURE 3.2.

## Overall conclusion

SOP-AIML-007 is **well designed for distributional drift**: it has defined drift metrics, continuous monitoring, retraining triggers, validation gates, rollback, and reporting. It also provides a credible MANAGE function response for known measurable degradation.

For **emergent risks**, the SOP is not fully complete. It handles some emergent-risk scenarios through manual triggers, adverse event escalation, incident response, and rollback, but it does not yet provide the full NIST AI RMF loop for unknown-risk tracking, user/impacted-community feedback, appeals, overrides, and stakeholder-informed measurement.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-AIML-019 (A/B Testing and Canary Releases for Models) satisfy NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function for production model changes?
question.expected_answer: Partial. The SOP implements canary releases and A/B testing infrastructure that supports gradual rollout — aligning with MANAGE 1 (risk-tier-driven prioritisation) and MANAGE 2 (risk response planning). However, MANAGE 4 specific mitigation procedures are described only at a high level — no explicit halt criteria tied to MEASURE outputs, no defined rollback thresholds for each trustworthiness characteristic, and stakeholder engagement during canaries is mentioned but lacks structured feedback mechanisms (no defined channels, cadence, or escalation path).
question.notes: Partial case grounded in matrix weaknesses.

output.answer:
Yes—but only **partially** if SOP-AIML-019 is assessed as a standalone procedure. It provides strong controls for **controlled production model changes** via A/B testing and canary releases, but it does not fully cover all NIST AI RMF MANAGE outcomes, especially broader post-deployment monitoring, third-party model risk, user/affected-community feedback, appeal/override, decommissioning, and residual-risk documentation.

| NIST AI RMF MANAGE requirement | Assessment of SOP-AIML-019 |
|---|---|
| **MANAGE 1.1 — Decide whether the AI system achieves intended purpose and whether deployment should proceed.** NIST requires a determination of whether the AI system achieves its intended purposes and stated objectives and whether development or deployment should proceed. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.1).* | **Satisfies.** SOP-AIML-019 requires a pre-registered Experiment Design Document with hypothesis, primary success metric, success criteria, guardrails, sizing, safety/ethical assessment, and rollback criteria before production traffic is routed. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§4, 5.1.1).* It also requires final DSRB review and CAIO Go/No-Go authorization before full rollout. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.4.1–5.4.2).* |
| **MANAGE 1.2–1.3 — Prioritize and document risk treatments based on impact, likelihood, and available methods.** NIST requires treatment of documented AI risks to be prioritized and responses to high-priority AI risks to be developed, planned, and documented. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.2–1.3).* | **Mostly satisfies for experiment/change risks.** The SOP requires guardrail metrics for high-risk models, safety/ethical assessment, rollback criteria, CSO review for high-risk clinical models, financial safety review for major financial models, and documented RCA after guardrail breaches. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§4 POL-019-03, 5.1.1, 5.1.3, 5.3.2).* However, it does not explicitly require documenting **residual risk** after rollout or formally classifying risk-treatment choices as mitigate/transfer/avoid/accept. |
| **MANAGE 1.4 — Document negative residual risks to downstream acquirers and end users.** NIST requires negative residual risks to downstream acquirers and end users to be documented. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.4).* | **Gap.** SOP-AIML-019 requires safety/ethical assessment and final results reporting, but it does not explicitly require a residual-risk statement for downstream users, acquirers, clinicians, patients, or other end users after the production change. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.1.1, 5.4.1).* |
| **MANAGE 2.1–2.2 — Plan and implement strategies to maximize benefits and sustain deployed-system value.** NIST requires resources, alternatives, and mechanisms to sustain value of deployed AI systems. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 2.1–2.2).* | **Partially satisfies.** The SOP requires statistically powered experiments, primary success metrics, practical-significance assessment, graduated canary gates, monitoring dashboards, rollback capability, and final rollout authorization. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.1.1, 5.3, 5.4.1–5.4.3, 6.1).* But it does not require documenting viable non-AI alternatives or alternative operational approaches as part of change approval. |
| **MANAGE 2.3 — Respond to and recover from newly identified risks.** NIST requires procedures to respond to and recover from previously unknown risks when identified. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 2.3).* | **Satisfies for live experiment risks.** The SOP requires automated pause on guardrail breach, CSO notification for high-risk models, RCA within 48 business hours, approval before resumption, automatic latency circuit breaker, zero-traffic kill switch, and emergency rollback escalation. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.3.2, 6.1, 8.2).* |
| **MANAGE 2.4 — Supersede, disengage, or deactivate AI systems with outcomes inconsistent with intended use.** NIST requires mechanisms and assigned responsibilities to supersede, disengage, or deactivate systems that demonstrate performance or outcomes inconsistent with intended use. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 2.4).* | **Mostly satisfies for rollback/deactivation during releases.** The SOP assigns RM, CAIO, and CSO authority for rollback/kill-switch actions and requires 0% treatment routing when guardrails breach. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§3.1, 5.3.2, 6.1, 8.2).* It is weaker on formal **decommissioning** of replaced models after full rollout, which NIST includes within the broader MANAGE function. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 4.1).* |
| **MANAGE 3.1–3.2 — Manage third-party AI risks and pre-trained models.** NIST requires third-party AI risks and benefits to be regularly monitored, with controls applied and documented, and requires pre-trained models used for development to be monitored as part of regular maintenance. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 3.1–3.2).* | **Gap / not addressed in this SOP.** SOP-AIML-019 applies to third-party vendors involved in experiments and requires vendor compliance generally, but it does not define third-party model/resource monitoring, third-party risk controls, or monitoring requirements for pre-trained models used in development. *(SOP-AIML-019 §1.3).* |
| **MANAGE 4.1 — Implement post-deployment monitoring plans, including user input, appeal/override, decommissioning, incident response, recovery, and change management.** NIST requires post-deployment monitoring plans with mechanisms for user/relevant AI actor input, appeal and override, decommissioning, incident response, recovery, and change management. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 4.1).* | **Partially satisfies.** SOP-AIML-019 strongly covers **change management**, experiment monitoring, incident response, recovery, rollback, audit trails, and stage-gated canary changes. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.2–5.4, 6.1, 6.2, 7.2, 8.2).* But it expressly excludes post-deployment monitoring for fully rolled-out models, assigning that to SOP-AIML-022. *(SOP-AIML-019 §1.2).* It also does not include appeal/override mechanisms or a formal user/affected-community feedback capture process. |
| **MANAGE 4.2 — Integrate measurable continual improvement into system updates with regular engagement with interested parties and relevant AI actors.** NIST requires measurable continual-improvement activities in updates and regular engagement with interested parties, including relevant AI actors. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 4.2).* | **Partially satisfies.** The SOP includes measurable improvement through primary success metrics, guardrails, false discovery rate tracking, experiment velocity, rollback duration, monthly health reporting, and quarterly governance review. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.1.1, 7.1–7.3).* However, regular engagement is mainly internal—EO, RM, DSRB, CSO, CAIO—and does not clearly require input from end users, patients, affected communities, or external AI actors. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§3, 7.3).* |
| **MANAGE 4.3 — Communicate incidents and errors to relevant AI actors and document tracking, response, and recovery.** NIST requires incidents and errors to be communicated to relevant AI actors, including affected communities, and requires tracking, response, and recovery processes to be followed and documented. *(NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 4.3).* | **Partially satisfies.** The SOP requires Slack alerts, RM pager notification, CSO notification for high-risk models, incident tickets, Guardrail Breach Reports, SEV1 escalation, and Splunk audit logging. *(SOP-AIML-019 §§5.3.2, 6.1, 8.2).* But it does not require communication to affected users, patients, customers, downstream acquirers, or affected communities when incidents/errors occur. |

**Bottom line:** SOP-AIML-019 is a strong production-change control for model experiments and canary releases. It aligns well with NIST AI RMF MANAGE outcomes for **deployment go/no-go decisions, risk response, rollback, change management, auditability, and incident recovery**. However, as a standalone SOP, it falls short of full MANAGE coverage because it excludes fully rolled-out post-deployment monitoring, lacks explicit residual-risk documentation, does not address third-party/pre-trained model risk management, and does not define mechanisms for appeal/override, decommissioning, or communication with affected communities and external AI actors.

Recommended remediation:
1. Add an explicit **Residual Risk Statement** requirement to the Experiment Results Report and final Go/No-Go package.  
2. Add a required handoff checklist to SOP-AIML-022 for post-rollout monitoring, including drift, fairness, safety, user feedback, and incident triggers.  
3. Add a third-party/pre-trained model section covering vendor-supplied models, foundation models, external APIs, and inherited model risks.  
4. Add user/affected-community communication requirements for material incidents, errors, or safety-related rollbacks.  
5. Add explicit appeal/override and decommissioning references or cross-links to relevant operational procedures.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-ITOP-005 (Capacity Planning and Management) compliant with SOC 2's incident-response and change-management expectations?
question.expected_answer: Partial. The capacity-planning programme covers forecasting, monitoring, and scaling actions, but two gaps remain: (1) the incident-response plan exists but tabletop exercises and scenario testing are not on a defined schedule (CC7.4/7.5 expects periodic testing), and (2) capacity-related change procedures lack segregation of duties between approver and implementer (CC8.1). Both are operational specificity gaps rather than missing controls.

output.answer:
SOP-ITOP-005 is **partially compliant** with SOC 2 expectations for incident response and change management. It has strong alignment for capacity-event detection, escalation, incident ticketing, root-cause review, change authorization, segregation of duties, and audit trails, but it has notable gaps around **periodic incident-response testing/tabletop exercises** and **explicit pre-production testing/emergency-change controls for capacity changes**.

## SOC 2 incident-response expectations

### What SOC 2 expects

SOC 2 requires organizations to monitor system components for anomalies and analyze whether anomalies represent security events under **CC7.2**. SOC 2 also expects detected events to be communicated and reviewed, incidents to be analyzed for system impact, and response procedures to be periodically evaluated under **CC7.3**. SOC 2 further requires a defined incident-response program to understand, contain, remediate, and communicate security incidents under **CC7.4**, and recovery activities to restore affected environments under **CC7.5**.

### Where SOP-ITOP-005 aligns

SOP-ITOP-005 aligns well with SOC 2’s detection and escalation expectations because it requires continuous monitoring, alert thresholds, ServiceNow ticketing, PagerDuty escalation, synthetic checks, and Kafka lag monitoring. This supports SOC 2 **CC7.2**, which requires monitoring system components and analyzing anomalies that may affect the entity’s objectives.

The SOP also supports incident evaluation and response because a capacity-related SLO breach must be declared an incident and managed under SOP-ISEC-002, with an Incident Commander documenting the root cause. This maps to SOC 2 **CC7.3**, which requires procedures to analyze incidents and determine system impact, and **CC7.4**, which requires executing a defined incident-response program.

The SOP’s post-incident review process is also a positive control. Section 5.5 requires a post-incident review within five business days of P1 or P2 capacity incident closure, including timeline analysis, monitoring review, alerting review, procedure execution review, and Jira action items. This supports SOC 2 **CC7.3**, which expects incidents to be reviewed and actions taken when necessary, and **CC7.4**, which expects remediation and response activities.

### Incident-response gap

The SOP explicitly states that regular tabletop exercises to rehearse response procedures are **not currently scheduled**. This is a compliance weakness because SOC 2 **CC7.3** expects the effectiveness of incident-response policies and procedures to be evaluated periodically, and SOC 2 **CC7.4** expects incident-response activities to be maintained and executed effectively. Post-incident reviews are useful, but they only test the process after real events; they do not provide periodic assurance when incidents do not occur.

**Required remediation:** Add a required cadence for incident-response testing, such as annual or semiannual tabletop exercises for capacity-driven outages, DDoS-related capacity exhaustion, cloud-region degradation, and emergency scaling scenarios. The SOP should require documented objectives, participants, results, gaps, owners, and remediation deadlines.

## SOC 2 change-management expectations

### What SOC 2 expects

SOC 2 **CC8.1** requires the entity to authorize, design, develop or acquire, configure, document, test, approve, and implement changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures. SOC 2 **CC8.1** also expects changes to be tracked before implementation, tested before production implementation, approved before implementation, deployed with segregation of responsibilities, and managed through an emergency-change process when urgent changes are necessary.

### Where SOP-ITOP-005 aligns

The SOP has several strong change-management controls. Section 4.4 requires production infrastructure capacity changes to be authorized through a ServiceNow Change Request and approved under SOP-CHG-001. This aligns with SOC 2 **CC8.1**, which requires changes to be authorized, documented, tracked, and approved before implementation.

The SOP also supports segregation of duties. Section 4.4 states that the implementer and approver must not be the same person, and Section 6.2 describes GitHub branch protection, CODEOWNERS review, restricted approvals, and ServiceNow enforcement that blocks the same user from being both implementer and approver. This aligns with SOC 2 **CC8.1**, which expects deployment of changes with segregation of responsibilities to prevent or detect unauthorized changes.

The use of Infrastructure as Code, pull requests, Terraform plans, Helm diffs, approval workflows, and ServiceNow records provides useful auditability and supports SOC 2 **CC8.1** requirements for documenting, tracking, configuring, approving, and implementing system changes.

The SOP also includes emergency-related mechanisms, including PagerDuty incident context for break-glass AWS console access, CloudTrail logging, Security Hub findings, exception requests, risk assessment by the Information Security Officer, and CTO escalation authority. These partially support SOC 2 **CC8.1**, which expects a process for emergency changes.

### Change-management gaps

The SOP does not consistently demonstrate **pre-production testing before implementation** for all capacity changes. Horizontal scaling includes Terraform plan or Helm diff and post-change validation, but it does not explicitly require testing before production implementation. Vertical scaling requires a maintenance window, rollback plan, and approval, but it also does not clearly require pre-production testing or validation in staging before production. SOC 2 **CC8.1** specifically expects system changes to be tested before implementation into production.

There is also a potential inconsistency in the horizontal scaling runbook. Section 4.4 says any production capacity change must be authorized through a ServiceNow Change Request, but the horizontal scaling procedure mainly references a pull request and later updating the ServiceNow Incident/Alert record. SOC 2 **CC8.1** expects changes to be tracked and approved before implementation, so the runbook should explicitly require a ServiceNow Change Request or approved emergency-change record before merging the production PR, unless the change is fully automated under a pre-approved standard-change model.

The emergency-change process is present but not fully specified for SOC 2 purposes. SOC 2 **CC8.1** expects emergency changes to be authorized, designed, tested, approved, and implemented in an urgent timeframe. SOP-ITOP-005 includes exceptions, break-glass logging, and CTO escalation, but it should more explicitly define emergency change approval, required minimum testing, retrospective review, evidence retention, and conversion of emergency actions into formal change records.

**Required remediation:** Update the SOP to require:
1. Pre-production testing or documented risk-based testing for all non-standard capacity changes.
2. Explicit ServiceNow Change Request linkage in every scaling runbook, including horizontal scaling.
3. A defined emergency-change workflow covering authorization, minimum testing, approval, implementation, post-implementation review, and evidence retention.
4. Periodic review of capacity-related changes and incidents for patterns and required control improvements.

## Bottom line

SOP-ITOP-005 is close to SOC 2 alignment and contains many well-designed controls, especially around monitoring, escalation, incident ticketing, IaC-based changes, approvals, segregation of duties, and post-incident reviews. However, it is not fully compliant because it lacks scheduled incident-response exercises and does not clearly require pre-production testing and complete emergency-change handling for all capacity-related production changes.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-LEGC-001 (Regulatory Change Management) compliant with GDPR's transparency and international-transfer requirements?
question.expected_answer: Partial. The SOP acknowledges international transfers and tracks GDPR developments, but two gaps remain: (1) Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and transfer-impact-assessment procedures are not explicitly referenced as the operational mechanism under Chapter V; and (2) the privacy-notice update workflow tied to regulatory change exists but does not enumerate the Articles 13/14 elements that must be reflected (purposes, lawful basis, recipients, retention, rights, transfers). Direction is right; operational specifics are missing.

output.answer:
## Short answer

SOP-LEGC-001 is **partially compliant** with GDPR transparency and international-transfer expectations as a **regulatory change management process**, but it is **not sufficient by itself** to demonstrate operational compliance with GDPR transparency duties or Chapter V transfer rules.

It provides a good governance wrapper—horizon scanning, DPO involvement, impact assessments, data lineage, control validation, and audit trails—but it does **not contain the substantive procedures/checklists needed to ensure privacy notices, data-subject communications, records of processing, transfer mechanisms, or safeguards are actually updated and validated when GDPR changes occur**.

---

## GDPR transparency requirements vs. SOP coverage

| GDPR requirement | What GDPR requires | SOP-LEGC-001 coverage | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transparent communications | Controllers must provide GDPR information and rights communications in a “concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form,” using clear and plain language. | The SOP requires “transparent translation of regulatory text into implementable technical controls” and audit traceability, but this is internal governance language, not data-subject-facing transparency. SOP §§1.1, 4 POL-LEGC-001-C. | **Gap** — no privacy-notice or data-subject communication standard aligned to GDPR Art. 12. |
| Articles 13/14 notice content | Privacy information must include controller/DPO details, purposes, legal basis, recipients, retention, rights, complaints, and, where applicable, international-transfer details and safeguards. | The SOP does not define a GDPR privacy notice content checklist or require that privacy notices be updated when regulatory changes affect processing. | **Gap** — no explicit Article 13/14 notice update workflow. |
| Information on international transfers in notices | Where transfers occur, data subjects must be told of the transfer, adequacy status, safeguards, and how to obtain a copy or where safeguards are available. | The SOP mentions “updated international data transfer procedures and privacy notice requirements” only in the revision history, but the operative procedure does not specify notice content, ownership, or validation. SOP §11 Revision History v2.7. | **Gap** — revision history alone is not an enforceable control. |
| Data-subject access transparency | Access responses must include recipients, including recipients in third countries or international organisations, and where transfers occur, the safeguards under Article 46. | The SOP does not address data-subject access responses or how transfer-safeguard information is provided on request. | **Gap** — no Article 15 access-response linkage. |
| DPO involvement | The DPO must advise and monitor GDPR compliance; the DPO must be involved properly and in a timely manner in personal-data issues. | The SOP identifies a DPO and requires the DPO to be consulted/informed on all RCIs impacting personal data; the DPO is included in the Impact Assessment Working Group if personal data is involved. SOP §§3, 5.2.1. | **Partial strength** — aligns procedurally with GDPR Arts. 38 and 39, but does not define DPO sign-off for privacy notices or transfer mechanisms. |

**Key GDPR citations:** GDPR Art. 12 requires concise, transparent, intelligible, easily accessible information; GDPR Arts. 13 and 14 require privacy notice content, including third-country transfer information and safeguards; GDPR Art. 15 requires informing data subjects of recipients in third countries and Article 46 safeguards; GDPR Arts. 38–39 require timely DPO involvement and DPO monitoring/advisory duties.

---

## GDPR international-transfer requirements vs. SOP coverage

| GDPR requirement | What GDPR requires | SOP-LEGC-001 coverage | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chapter V transfer baseline | Transfers to third countries or international organisations may occur only if GDPR Chapter V conditions are met, including onward transfers. | SOP includes GDPR in scope and requires regulatory changes to be assessed, remediated, validated, and traced to source provisions.
